reflection_text: From this week's lecture on drug shortages, I learned that shortages stem from a combination of structural vulnerabilities: heavy reliance on single-source manufacturers, low profit margins that discourage investment in generic injectable production, and the fragility of global supply chains for active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) — many of which originate from a small number of countries. I also learned about which drug categories are most at risk, particularly sterile injectables and generic medications, and about the ethical frameworks pharmacists use to allocate limited supply, including considerations of clinical urgency and equity. This matters deeply to me as a future pharmacist because it reframes how I think about the medications I will dispense. Before this lecture, I assumed drug shortages were rare exceptions; now I understand they are a predictable, recurring feature of the healthcare system. This shift was uncomfortable but important — it means clinical competence alone is not enough; I must also develop systems-level awareness and proactive communication skills. The discussion of ethical allocation particularly challenged my assumption that distribution decisions are straightforward when supply is limited. In future rotations and practice, I plan to build shortage awareness into my daily workflow by routinely consulting the ASHP Drug Shortages database and FDA shortage list to anticipate supply disruptions before they reach the patient. When a shortage requires a therapeutic interchange, I will follow a structured approach: verify the clinical equivalence of available alternatives, identify differences in dosing, route of administration, and monitoring requirements, and communicate these changes clearly to the prescribing team. For patients, I will explain the shortage in plain language, outline the available options, and document the rationale for the selected alternative. I will also advocate within my institution for shortage management protocols that incorporate equity considerations, ensuring limited supply is distributed based on clinical need rather than access or purchasing power.

I learned that shortages are driven by factors including limited manufacturing capacity, quality failures such as contamination at production facilities, over-reliance on single suppliers for active pharmaceutical ingredients, and low market profitability for generic medications — particularly sterile injectables. I also learned that pharmacists play a central role in shortage management, including identifying therapeutic alternatives and supporting ethical allocation decisions when supply is critically limited. Knowing this will be relevant to my future career, since I may regularly encounter situations where first-line medications are unavailable. In the future, I will try to stay informed about current drug shortages and work with providers to find appropriate alternatives when medications are unavailable. I hope to apply what I learned in my upcoming rotations.

reflection_text: This week I gained a much more nuanced understanding of the structural causes of drug shortages. I learned that the generic drug market — especially for sterile injectables — operates on such thin profit margins that manufacturers have little financial incentive to invest in redundant production capacity or rapid quality remediation. When a manufacturer receives an FDA warning letter or encounters a contamination event, they may face months-long production halts with no other producer able to absorb the volume. Compounding this, API sourcing is highly concentrated: a significant proportion of APIs used in U.S. drug products originate from a small number of facilities, primarily in India and China, creating geopolitical and logistical vulnerabilities. I also learned about specific shortage management strategies, including allocation protocols, therapeutic interchanges, and conservation practices such as dose optimization. This lecture challenged a belief I had held without examining: that if a drug is on the market, it will generally be available. Understanding the root causes of shortage makes me realize that drug availability is not guaranteed, and that pharmacists are among the frontline professionals responsible for managing this uncertainty safely. This matters for my development because it adds a layer of complexity to clinical decision-making that I had not fully appreciated — I will need to consider not just what is clinically optimal, but also what is actually accessible, and how to protect patients when optimal options are unavailable. I saw a version of this during my IPPE rotation when a patient's antiepileptic medication was on shortage and the team had to urgently identify an alternative and carefully monitor the transition. Going forward, I will incorporate shortage surveillance into my professional practice by setting up alerts through the ASHP and FDA shortage databases. When managing a therapeutic interchange, I will systematically evaluate the alternative for pharmacokinetic equivalence, dosing and administration differences, new monitoring parameters, and patient-specific contraindications, and document my clinical rationale clearly in the patient record. In community practice, I will proactively contact the prescriber when a drug is unavailable rather than waiting for the patient to discover it at pick-up, and I will explain the situation and proposed alternative in plain, reassuring language. I also intend to participate in institutional shortage committees as I advance in my career, contributing to allocation policies grounded in clinical need and equity.

reflection_text: The most significant learning from this week's drug shortage lecture was understanding the interplay between market economics and patient safety. Specifically, I learned that the U.S. drug shortage problem is driven by several reinforcing factors: the race-to-the-bottom economics of generic manufacturing, where profit margins are so thin that a single recall or FDA enforcement action can eliminate a company's ability to produce a critical medication; the consolidation of drug manufacturing into a small number of large facilities; and chronic underinvestment in domestic API production. I also learned about practical shortage management frameworks used by pharmacists, including the ASHP drug shortage management guidelines, which outline steps from shortage identification and impact assessment to therapeutic interchange selection and communication planning. Finally, I learned that oncology agents and sterile injectables are disproportionately affected, which has serious implications for vulnerable patient populations who cannot tolerate treatment delays. This information changed the way I think about my future role. I had previously thought of therapeutic interchange as an inconvenient workaround for edge cases; now I understand it as a core clinical skill that pharmacists may exercise regularly, sometimes under time pressure, and with real consequences for patient outcomes. The fact that chemotherapy drugs are frequently in shortage particularly struck me: these are patients for whom a treatment delay can have life-altering consequences. The ethical weight of deciding who receives the available supply — and on what basis — is something I had not grappled with before this lecture, and it made me think of my responsibility not just as a drug expert but as an advocate for equitable access. For my upcoming hospital rotations, I will ask preceptors about shortage management protocols in place at each institution and actively participate in reviewing any current shortages on the unit. I will practice running through a therapeutic interchange evaluation for at least one shortage scenario per rotation — confirming pharmacologic class, comparing pharmacokinetic profiles, identifying dose conversion requirements, and flagging new monitoring parameters. I will also draft patient-friendly explanations of common shortage scenarios and refine them with preceptor feedback. After graduation, I intend to contribute to my institution's Pharmacy and Therapeutics committee, helping develop shortage contingency plans informed by both clinical evidence and equity principles.

I learned that shortages rarely have a single cause; instead, they result from overlapping vulnerabilities in the pharmaceutical supply chain — including heavy API sourcing from a limited number of overseas facilities, the economics of generic drug production that discourage manufacturers from maintaining excess capacity, and the slow recovery time following FDA quality enforcement actions. I also learned about the categories of medications most at risk, particularly sterile injectables and narrow-therapeutic-index drugs, and about the tools pharmacists use to respond, including therapeutic interchange protocols and shortage allocation frameworks. reflection_text: From this week's lecture I learned that drug shortages are a predictable, recurring consequence of structural vulnerabilities in the U.S. pharmaceutical supply chain. Key drivers include the consolidation of generic drug manufacturing among a small number of facilities operating on thin profit margins; heavy dependence on overseas API sources from a limited number of countries, creating single points of failure; and the FDA regulatory environment, in which enforcement actions — though necessary for quality — can trigger prolonged supply disruptions when no alternative manufacturer exists. I also learned about the disproportionate impact on sterile injectables, oncology agents, and critical care medications, and about specific tools pharmacists use during shortages: the ASHP and FDA shortage databases, therapeutic interchange protocols, conservation strategies such as dose optimization, and ethical allocation frameworks for critically limited supplies. This lecture fundamentally shifted how I think about drug availability. I had previously taken for granted that if a medication was clinically appropriate, it would generally be accessible. Now I understand that drug access is a managed resource that requires active attention. This matters because it broadens what clinical competence means: a skilled pharmacist must know not just what the best therapy is, but whether it will be available and have a plan for when it is not. This connects to values I hold around health equity — the lecture raised the uncomfortable reality that shortages often hit safety-net institutions and underserved populations hardest, because they lack the purchasing power and storage capacity of larger academic medical centers. That systemic inequity is something I want to actively work against in my career. In my upcoming rotations, I will begin each clinical day by checking the ASHP Drug Shortages database for medications relevant to that practice setting and discuss any active shortages with my preceptor. When I encounter a therapeutic interchange scenario, I will work through a structured evaluation: verifying pharmacologic equivalence, identifying dose conversion factors, assessing differences in monitoring parameters and adverse effect profiles, and documenting my clinical rationale in the patient chart. I will practice communicating shortage situations to providers in a solution-oriented way — not just flagging a problem, but presenting a vetted alternative with supporting rationale. For community practice, I will prepare patient-centered shortage conversations that explain why the shortage has occurred, what alternative is available, and what differences the patient should be aware of, in language that is reassuring rather than alarming.

reflection_text:
Today's session looked at how pharmacists communicate with patients when a prescribed medication is on shortage, and what makes that conversation succeed or fail. I learned that the most common failure mode is the "silent substitution" — filling an alternative without explaining the change — which patients often discover at home and which erodes trust in the entire pharmacy. We reviewed a structured approach: confirm the shortage with the wholesaler and verify therapeutic equivalence, contact the prescriber before counseling so a documented plan exists, then explain to the patient (1) what the shortage is, (2) what the alternative is and why it is appropriate, (3) any differences in administration, side effects, or cost they should expect, and (4) what to do if the original drug becomes available again.

This mattered to me because I had not appreciated how much of shortage management is fundamentally a communication problem rather than a procurement problem. A patient who feels surprised or dismissed during a shortage will avoid future fills, skip doses, or seek out unverified online suppliers — all of which can harm them more than the shortage itself. The session reframed for me that the pharmacist's clinical reasoning during a shortage only protects the patient if it is translated into clear, calm language they can act on.

I will apply this at my community internship by (1) drafting a short "shortage script" with my preceptor that I can adapt for the most commonly affected classes (ADHD stimulants, GLP-1s, injectable antibiotics); (2) committing to always document the prescriber contact and counseling in the patient's profile rather than relying on memory; and (3) following up with at least one patient by phone after a shortage substitution during my first month to learn directly what was unclear in my counseling.

This week we discussed how pharmacists should communicate with patients when a medication is on shortage. The main idea is that patients need to be told clearly when their medication is being substituted, why, and what the differences are. We talked about how silent substitutions damage trust and can lead patients to stop taking their medication or look for it elsewhere.

This is important because communication during a shortage is part of patient safety, not just an inconvenience. If patients don't understand the change, they may not take the new medication correctly. At my internship I will try to be clear with patients when there are substitutions and explain what they need to know.

reflection_text:
This session focused on the formal infrastructure that the U.S. uses to detect, report, and respond to drug shortages — specifically the FDA's Drug Shortage Staff, the FDA's required manufacturer notification rules under the Food and Drug Administration Safety and Innovation Act (FDASIA), and the parallel ASHP Drug Shortages Resource Center, which is curated by clinical pharmacists and is often more actionable for hospital practice. I learned that FDASIA requires manufacturers to notify the FDA at least six months in advance of a discontinuation or interruption in supply for medically necessary drugs, and that this advance notice gives the FDA time to coordinate with other manufacturers, expedite inspections of alternate facilities, and exercise regulatory discretion (e.g., temporary importation) when needed.

What stood out to me is the difference in purpose between the FDA list and the ASHP list. The FDA list is regulatory and tells you whether a shortage is officially recognized, whereas the ASHP list reflects on-the-ground availability and includes practical management recommendations from practicing pharmacists. I had previously assumed the two were redundant; today's class clarified that experienced pharmacists check both, and that knowing why each exists changes how you use it.

I plan to apply this by (1) bookmarking both the FDA Drug Shortages page and the ASHP Drug Shortages Resource Center on my pharmacy laptop and reviewing them weekly during my hospital rotation; (2) asking my preceptor to walk me through how our institution's drug shortage team triages a new entry on the ASHP list — who decides on substitution policy and how it gets communicated to prescribers; and (3) signing up for the ASHP Drug Shortages email alerts so I can build the habit of monitoring before it becomes a job requirement.

reflection_text:
This week's session focused on the clinical decision-making involved in selecting therapeutic alternatives when a drug is on shortage. I learned that not all substitutions are pharmacologically equivalent — the same therapeutic class can differ in onset, half-life, monitoring requirements, and renal/hepatic adjustments. We worked through a piperacillin-tazobactam shortage case where the substitution options (cefepime + metronidazole vs. meropenem) carried different antimicrobial stewardship implications, different rates of C. difficile risk, and different cost profiles. The discussion emphasized that an evidence-based substitution requires considering the indication, the patient's allergies and organ function, the site's antibiogram, and stewardship goals — not just "another drug in the same bucket."

This shifted my thinking because I had implicitly assumed that therapeutic substitution during a shortage was largely a procurement decision. In reality, every substitution is a clinical decision that the pharmacist owns. Going through the case made me appreciate why hospital P&T committees pre-authorize specific substitution pathways during shortages — so that high-stakes choices are not made in real time at the dispensing window without input from infectious disease or stewardship pharmacists.

I will apply this at my internship by (1) asking to review the inpatient pharmacy's pre-approved substitution pathways for the three most commonly shortage-affected drug classes at the site; (2) sitting in on a P&T committee meeting if a shortage agenda item comes up to observe how the substitution policy is debated; and (3) drafting a one-page comparison table for one current shortage drug at my site, including indication, stewardship considerations, and monitoring differences, that I can have my preceptor critique.

reflection_text:
This session addressed something I had not previously considered as part of pharmacy practice: the ethics of allocating a drug that is in critical shortage. We reviewed real cases — pediatric oncology agents, IV fluids during the 2017 Hurricane Maria shortage, and tocilizumab during COVID-19 — and the frameworks hospitals have used to decide who receives the limited supply. The core principles we discussed were transparency (allocation criteria are documented and disclosable), consistency (the same criteria apply to every patient), and clinical justification (allocation is based on indication and expected benefit rather than ability to pay or first-come-first-served). We also discussed the role of an ethics committee and why allocation decisions should not rest solely with the pharmacist on shift.

This learning mattered to me because I realized I had been thinking of shortages as a logistics problem with a clinical wrapper. In reality, when supply truly cannot meet demand, the pharmacist becomes part of an explicit moral choice about which patient is treated. The session also clarified that allocation fairness is not the same as allocation neutrality — "first come first served" feels neutral but systematically disadvantages patients with less healthcare access. That distinction was uncomfortable but important.

I plan to apply this by (1) asking my hospital preceptor whether the institution has a written drug allocation policy and, if so, requesting permission to read it; (2) attending or shadowing one ethics committee meeting during my rotation, even if shortage allocation is not the topic, to understand how the committee deliberates; and (3) when I encounter my first true allocation decision, deliberately escalating to my preceptor or the institution's policy rather than deciding alone, even if I think I know the right answer.

reflection_text:
Today's session focused on what hospital and 503A pharmacies can do when a commercial product is unavailable: compound the same drug from raw active pharmaceutical ingredient (API) under USP <797> and <800> standards. I learned the regulatory boundary clearly — 503A pharmacies may compound a drug that is in shortage even if it is essentially a copy of a commercial product, but only while the drug is on the FDA shortage list, and only with a patient-specific prescription. We walked through the operational requirements: sourcing API from an FDA-registered facility, verifying certificates of analysis for identity and potency, assigning beyond-use dates based on USP category and stability data, and documenting the compounding record so it can withstand a board inspection.

What this clarified for me was the limits of pharmacy compounding as a shortage response. I had vaguely thought of compounding as a broad workaround for any out-of-stock drug. The class made clear that the regulatory framework is narrower and more conditional than I assumed, and that compounding from API carries patient-safety risks that commercial manufacturing doesn't — variability in potency, sterility failures, and lack of long-term stability data. Realizing this gave me a more honest view of when compounding solves a shortage and when it just shifts the risk.

I will apply this by (1) asking my hospital preceptor to walk me through one compounded shortage workaround the institution has used in the past year and the documentation chain that supported it; (2) reviewing one product's certificate of analysis with my preceptor so I understand what to look for before working with API independently; and (3) building a personal mental checklist — FDA shortage status, prescription specificity, BUD assignment, stability — that I run through whenever I am asked to compound from API.

reflection_text:
This session focused specifically on shortages of large-volume parenteral products — saline, dextrose, lactated Ringer's — and what makes this category uniquely difficult to manage. I learned that the U.S. has only a small number of sterile injectable manufacturers (Baxter, B. Braun, ICU Medical) and that a single facility outage (e.g., the 2017 Hurricane Maria impact on Baxter's Puerto Rico plants) can produce a national IV fluid shortage that lasts months. Hospitals respond with conservation strategies: switching from 100 mL minibags to IV push for compatible drugs, using oral hydration when clinically appropriate, and rotating allocations across units. We also discussed why sterile injectable manufacturing is so concentrated — the capital cost of a compliant fill-finish line is high and margins are thin, so few entrants are viable.

What this clarified for me is how invisible the IV fluid supply is until it fails. I had treated saline as essentially free and infinite. The session reframed it as the most concentrated single-point-of-failure in modern hospital pharmacy. The personal piece was realizing that an IV push protocol for what used to be a minibag drug is a real clinical change with real risks (extravasation, dosing errors, nursing workflow), and that the pharmacist is the team member most likely to identify those risks early.

I will apply this by (1) asking my hospital preceptor whether the institution has a written conservation protocol for IV fluids and reviewing it before any shortage hits; (2) shadowing one nursing shift on a unit using IV push for a former minibag drug to observe how the workflow change actually plays out at the bedside; and (3) keeping a personal note of which institutional shortage responses I see work and which create new risks, so I am building practical knowledge for when I am responsible for proposing them.

reflection_text:
Today's class examined the gray market for shortage drugs — secondary distributors who buy from authorized wholesalers during stable supply and resell at substantial markup when shortages emerge. I learned that gray-market purchases pose three risks: dramatically inflated prices (sometimes 10x to 100x), uncertain provenance and chain of custody (which the DSCSA framework is meant to prevent), and the moral hazard of giving distributors an incentive to hoard. We discussed the ASHP guideline that hospital pharmacies should purchase from authorized distributors only, and the operational reality that during severe shortages — particularly oncology drugs and code-cart essentials — even responsible institutions have at times sourced gray-market product to keep patients alive. The class also addressed hoarding by individual hospitals, which transfers shortage from one institution to another without solving the underlying supply problem.

This shifted how I think about institutional purchasing decisions during shortages. I had not understood that what looks like a procurement choice is often an ethics choice — buying from gray market may save the patient in front of you while harming patients elsewhere whose institutions cannot pay the premium. Recognizing the pattern is what makes it possible to consider alternatives like regional collaboration and equitable allocation agreements.

I will apply this by (1) asking my hospital preceptor whether the institution has a written policy on gray-market purchasing during shortages and what thresholds trigger consideration; (2) reading the ASHP gray-market guidance in full so I can engage credibly when this comes up in P&T discussions; and (3) when I see purchase orders during my inpatient rotation, paying attention to which distributors the institution uses and asking why.

reflection_text:
Today's session looked at how drug shortages disproportionately harm vulnerable populations — specifically pediatric oncology and rare-disease patients — and what that pattern implies for pharmacy practice. I learned that pediatric oncology has been hit harder than adult oncology by recent shortages because pediatric formulations and trials are often smaller markets with fewer manufacturers. The vincristine and methotrexate shortages of recent years forced regimen substitutions in curable-intent treatment protocols, raising real questions about long-term cure rates. We also reviewed the rare-disease angle: when a shortage hits a drug used for a small population, alternatives may not exist at all, and the patient's options narrow to compassionate use or going without.

This learning was important to me because I had been thinking of shortages in aggregate terms — hospital response, allocation policy, communication. The session re-grounded the issue in particular patients whose outcomes depend on whether the right drug is on the shelf. The personal piece for me was a sharpened sense of what is at stake in shortage management: a child's cure rate or a rare-disease patient's quality of life is not abstract.

I will apply this by (1) requesting an oncology rotation week if my program permits, with a specific focus on how the team handles regimen substitution during shortages; (2) asking my preceptor to share one example of a shortage-driven regimen change and what the team's reasoning was; and (3) committing to bring the vulnerable-population framing into any P&T or stewardship discussion I'm part of, because shortage decisions made in general terms often hit specific patients hardest.

reflection_text:
This session covered the FDA's regulatory tools for managing shortages that go beyond manufacturer engagement: temporary importation of foreign-approved equivalents, regulatory discretion (e.g., extending expiration dates after stability testing), and expanded access pathways for older off-patent drugs that no manufacturer is willing to produce. I learned that the FDA's Importation Program identifies foreign-approved versions of a shortage drug (typically from EU regulators with comparable standards) and authorizes controlled importation, often via specific U.S. distributors. We also reviewed the critical detail that imported product carries its foreign labeling and patient-information leaflets in the foreign language, so pharmacist counseling and dispensing labels become the patient's only English-language information source.

What this changed for me was a sense of how dynamic and creative shortage regulation actually is. I had been thinking of FDA in static, rule-bound terms. This session made clear that during a real shortage the agency has discretionary tools that can materially expand supply, and that pharmacists are the team that operationalizes those tools at the dispensing window. Imported product in particular reframes the counseling encounter — the patient cannot read the product's own label, so the pharmacist's verbal counseling and English auxiliary labeling are the actual safety net.

I plan to apply this by (1) reviewing the FDA Importation Program web page and noting which drugs are currently authorized for import; (2) asking my preceptor whether the institution has dispensed any imported product in the past year and, if so, how the labeling and counseling were handled; and (3) if I encounter an imported product during my rotation, deliberately taking extra time on counseling and producing a written English summary the patient can take home.

reflection_text:
Today's class examined how a hospital P&T (Pharmacy and Therapeutics) committee handles shortages — specifically the formulary decisions that translate a national supply problem into local clinical practice. I learned that a well-functioning P&T shortage workflow has four steps: detect (alert from wholesaler or FDA list reaches the shortage coordinator), assess (stewardship and clinical pharmacists evaluate alternatives), decide (P&T approves substitution pathway including dosing equivalents and documentation), and communicate (prescribers, nurses, and frontline pharmacy all receive consistent guidance). The committee also tracks balance measures: is the substitution producing more adverse drug events, more LOS, more cost? We discussed that institutions without this structure default to ad-hoc substitutions that vary by shift and unit — which is unsafe.

What this learning gave me was a concrete picture of where pharmacy leadership lives during a shortage. Before today, I had thought of formulary management as a quiet committee activity. The session reframed it as the central operational nervous system that converts a chaotic supply event into an orderly clinical response. I also realized that my voice as a future pharmacist matters most upstream in this process — the clinical reasoning behind the substitution pathway shapes every patient touched by the shortage.

I will apply this by (1) attending one P&T meeting during my hospital rotation, ideally one with a shortage agenda item, to observe the deliberation; (2) asking my preceptor to share one substitution pathway document so I can study the level of detail expected; and (3) committing, when I am eventually in a hospital staff role, to volunteer for the shortage team early — that is the highest-leverage place for a junior pharmacist to learn institutional decision-making.

reflection_text:
This session covered the technology layer of shortage management — EHR alerts, shortage-tracking software (e.g., Vizient, IQVIA, the McKesson tools), and how these integrate with the dispensing workflow to surface the right information at the right moment. I learned that an effective shortage alert fires at order entry (not at dispensing), pulls in the institution's approved substitution pathway, and routes documentation to the patient's chart automatically. We also reviewed the pitfalls: alert fatigue from over-firing, stale alerts that persist after the shortage resolves, and systems that alert the prescriber but not the verifying pharmacist (so the substitution happens silently). The class emphasized that technology amplifies a good P&T process and replaces a bad one only at risk.

What this clarified for me is that shortage management technology is only valuable to the extent that the underlying clinical and operational decisions have already been made. A shortage alert that pops up without an institutional substitution pathway behind it just adds noise. The personal piece was realizing that pharmacy informatics and operations are interdependent — I had been thinking of them as separate career tracks, but a pharmacist working in either one needs to understand the other.

I will apply this by (1) asking my hospital preceptor what shortage-tracking tools the institution uses and requesting a demo; (2) reviewing the current configured shortage alerts in the EHR and noting which seem stale or low-value; and (3) following up with the medication safety officer (or pharmacy informatics team) on at least one alert that I think could be improved, so I am practicing the feedback loop between operations and IT.

reflection_text:
This final session on drug shortages took a step back to ask why the U.S. has had a drug shortage problem for over twenty years and what would actually prevent shortages rather than respond to them. I learned that the durable solutions sit upstream of any pharmacy intervention: investing in redundant manufacturing capacity, reforming the generic injectable market so that quality (not just price) is rewarded, requiring transparency in manufacturing locations and quality histories, and incentivizing domestic or allied-country production for critical drugs. We also reviewed the policy tools being explored — Section 3 of the CARES Act, FDA's Quality Management Maturity rating program, and Operation Warp Speed-style production investments for non-pandemic drugs.

This was important because I realized I had been thinking of shortages entirely as something for pharmacists to manage. The session reframed it as a market and policy problem that pharmacists also have to advocate about. Pharmacy professional organizations like ASHP have been the loudest voices for structural reform precisely because pharmacists see the patient harm first. That changes my picture of what professional engagement looks like — it isn't separate from clinical practice; it's an extension of it.

I will apply this by (1) joining ASHP and reading their drug shortage policy positions before my first job; (2) signing up for at least one advocacy alert system so I can respond to shortage-related federal regulatory comment periods when they open; and (3) when I am asked about shortages by patients, prescribers, or news outlets in my career, framing my response to include the structural causes rather than treating each shortage as an isolated event.

reflection_text: In today's session on high-alert medications (HAMs), I learned that HAMs are medications that bear a heightened risk of causing significant patient harm when used in error, and that the Institute for Safe Medication Practices (ISMP) publishes a widely-used list that groups them both by class (e.g., anticoagulants such as heparin and warfarin, insulins, opioids, neuromuscular blocking agents, and concentrated electrolytes such as potassium chloride) and by specific drug name. I also learned that The Joint Commission requires hospitals to maintain their own facility-specific HAM list tailored to prescribing patterns and patient populations, and to implement targeted risk-reduction strategies at every stage of the medication use process: prescribing, transcribing, dispensing, administering, and monitoring. We also reviewed the hierarchy of safety interventions — from high-leverage strategies like forcing functions (e.g., removing concentrated KCl from floor stock) and constraints, to medium-leverage strategies like standardization and barcoding, down to low-leverage strategies like education and reminders. This learning matters for my growth because it reframes how I think about patient safety. I used to assume that catching an error at the bedside through careful double-checking was the core of safe practice, but this session challenged that assumption by showing me that independent double-checks are a low-to-medium power strategy and can fail under time pressure or cognitive load. Understanding the power hierarchy pushes me to evaluate safety interventions more critically and to advocate for system-level fixes rather than relying on personal vigilance. It also deepens my appreciation for why a pharmacist's role extends beyond verification and into operational design. I will apply this learning during my hospital rotation by first asking my preceptor for the institutional HAM list and reviewing the specific safeguards in place — for example, smart-pump drug libraries for insulin infusions, tall-man lettering for look-alike/sound-alike HAMs, and mandatory independent double-checks for pediatric chemotherapy. When I encounter a HAM order, I will intentionally pause to verify indication, dose, route, and monitoring parameters before verifying it. If I identify a gap — for example, a HAM without an associated hard-stop in the order set — I will document it and bring it to my preceptor to discuss whether a higher-leverage safeguard could be added.

reflection_text: In this week's session, we discussed high-alert medications and how errors can happen at different points along the way. We talked about how hospitals try to reduce these risks using different strategies. This learning matters to me because it changed my understanding of where medication safety actually lives. I used to think about safety as happening at the final step — the pharmacist checking the order or the nurse administering the drug — but this session helped me realize that errors are often introduced much earlier, at the prescribing or transcribing stage, and the later steps are just the last chance to catch them. This is important for my development because it shifts my mental model from "catch errors at the end" to "design each step to make errors less likely in the first place." It also makes me more aware that my own future decisions as a pharmacist — the order sets I use, the clarifications I ask for, how I communicate with nurses — are all part of a shared system. I want to approach HAMs with that systems mindset rather than relying on individual carefulness. In the future, I will try to be mindful of HAMs when I see them at work.

reflection_text: In this week's session on high-alert medications (HAMs), I learned that errors involving HAMs can occur at any of the five stages of the medication use process — prescribing, transcribing, dispensing, administering, and monitoring — and that targeted safeguards need to be layered at each stage rather than concentrated at a single point. Specific examples we reviewed included dose-range checks and standardized concentration libraries at prescribing; computerized provider order entry (CPOE) with embedded clinical decision support to eliminate transcription; barcode medication administration (BCMA) and independent double-checks at dispensing and administering; and protocolized laboratory monitoring (e.g., anti-Xa or INR for anticoagulants, glucose checks for insulin drips) during monitoring. We also discussed how many sentinel events involving HAMs like heparin and insulin trace back to upstream issues such as look-alike packaging or ambiguous orders, rather than failures at the bedside. This learning matters for my growth because it reshapes how I conceptualize medication safety. Previously I assumed the pharmacist's main contribution was the verification step, but this session showed me that the most leverage actually comes from designing the upstream system — the order set, the product selection, the storage configuration — so that the dangerous action becomes difficult or impossible. It also challenged my assumption that errors reflect individual inattention; in reality, well-intentioned clinicians can be set up to fail by poorly designed processes. That insight is foundational to how I want to practice. I will apply this in my APPE rotations by asking to review at least one sentinel event or medication-error case involving a HAM at each site and tracing where along the medication use process the failure occurred. When I verify HAM orders, I will not just confirm the dose is appropriate but also note whether upstream safeguards like indication-linked order sets and concentration standardization were used. If I identify a recurring weak point — for example, nurses repeatedly calling to clarify a heparin protocol — I plan to bring it up in a P&T or medication safety committee meeting as a candidate for a system-level fix.

reflection_text: In today's session on high-alert medications (HAMs), I learned that a significant share of HAM errors stem from look-alike/sound-alike (LASA) confusion and packaging-related failures. ISMP and the FDA maintain confusable-name pair lists — for example, hydroxyzine vs. hydralazine, DOBUTamine vs. DOPamine, and insulin products with similar branding across concentrations (U-100 vs. U-500 regular insulin) — and recommend specific mitigations such as tall-man lettering (e.g., "DOBUTamine"/"DOPamine"), physical separation of storage bins, distinct auxiliary labels, and purchasing from different manufacturers when possible to avoid near-identical vials. We also discussed how unit-dose packaging, barcode scanning at dispensing and administration, and removal of concentrated electrolytes from floor stock have been shown in the literature to meaningfully reduce HAM errors at the system level. This matters for my development because it concretely illustrates that "being careful" is not sufficient protection against errors that are built into the physical environment. I had not previously thought about the vial label, the bin location, or even the typeface as safety interventions, but the session helped me see that these are exactly the high-leverage levers that remove reliance on memory and vigilance. This reframes the pharmacist's role as partly that of a human-factors designer: every choice about storage, labeling, and workflow is a safety decision. It also makes me more humble about my own susceptibility to LASA errors — I now understand that I can be well-trained and still mix up DOBUTamine and DOPamine if the system sets me up to. I will apply this at my hospital rotation by doing a walk-through of the automated dispensing cabinet and pharmacy shelves with my preceptor and identifying any LASA pairs that are stored adjacent to each other, flagging them for separation or additional labeling. I also plan to review our facility's tall-man lettering policy to check that it aligns with the current ISMP list, and to observe a BCMA workflow at administration to understand where an unscanned HAM would still be caught. If I see a near-miss involving a LASA HAM, I will write it up through the institution's event-reporting system so the pattern can be tracked and addressed.

reflection_text: In this week's class, I learned how safety strategies differ across specific high-alert medications based on the type of harm each one causes. For insulin, key safeguards include standardized U-100 concentration (with U-500 kept out of floor stock and dispensed in patient-specific doses only), bedside blood-glucose verification before administration, and avoiding the abbreviation "U" on handwritten orders because it is frequently misread as a zero and leads to ten-fold overdoses. For heparin, safeguards include standardized IV concentrations, weight-based dosing protocols with built-in maximums, anti-Xa or aPTT monitoring protocols, and separate storage of unfractionated heparin from low-molecular-weight heparin to prevent product mix-ups. For chemotherapy, safeguards include a two-pharmacist independent verification of the order and the compounded product, patient-specific body-surface-area (BSA) dose checks, verification of cumulative lifetime doses for agents like anthracyclines (to prevent irreversible cardiotoxicity), and administration only by credentialed oncology nurses. This learning matters for my growth because it teaches me that high-alert medication safety is not a single generic checklist — it is a set of drug-specific, failure-mode-specific defenses. Before this session I thought of HAMs as a homogeneous category that required uniform extra care. Now I understand that the "extra care" is shaped by the pharmacology and the most likely error for that specific drug: hypoglycemia from insulin, bleeding from heparin, irreversible cardiotoxicity from anthracyclines. That insight pushes me to learn each HAM's mechanism and failure modes deeply rather than treating them as interchangeable. I will apply this during my hospital IPPE and APPE rotations by building a personal reference for the top five HAMs at each site — what the most common errors are, what safeguards exist at that institution, and where the safeguards have known gaps. When I verify an insulin or heparin order, I will explicitly check the concentration, weight-based dose, and indication against the protocol rather than relying only on whether the order "looks reasonable." For chemotherapy, I will shadow the oncology pharmacist's independent verification process and practice the checks under supervision before performing them myself.

reflection_text:
Today's session focused on the independent double-check (IDC) as a high-alert medication safeguard, and on the difference between an effective IDC and a performative one. I learned that an independent double-check requires two qualified individuals to independently verify the patient, drug, dose, concentration, route, rate, and infusion programming without one priming the other (e.g., not saying "this is heparin 25,000 units, can you check?" but rather presenting only the order and the prepared product so the second checker must arrive at the verification independently). We reviewed ISMP guidance that IDCs are most appropriate for narrow-therapeutic-index HAMs (insulin, opioid infusions, chemotherapy, neuromuscular blockers) and not as a universal control — overusing IDCs erodes their effectiveness because nurses begin to rubber-stamp checks they perform constantly.

This learning shifted how I think about pharmacy safety controls. I had previously considered "more checks = safer" without recognizing that a poorly designed double-check can give false reassurance and crowd out attention to other safeguards. The distinction between an independent check and a confirmation check was the most useful piece — it explained to me why some hospitals report that their IDC compliance rate is high but their HAM error rate has not improved.

I will apply this on my hospital rotation by (1) observing at least three independent double-check events with my preceptor and noting whether they meet ISMP's independence criteria or are confirmation checks in disguise; (2) asking the medication safety officer how the institution decides which medications trigger an IDC and how that list is reviewed; and (3) practicing the technique of presenting only the order and the product when I am the first checker, so that I am building the habit before I am responsible for the verification independently.

reflection_text:
This session covered smart infusion pump technology and dose error reduction systems (DERS) as engineering controls for high-alert medication safety. I learned that smart pumps house a customizable drug library — created and maintained by the pharmacy — that includes hard limits (which the pump will not let the user override) and soft limits (which alert but allow override with documented reason). The library is segmented by clinical care area (adult ICU, NICU, oncology) so that, for example, a heparin concentration that is acceptable in adult cardiac is hard-blocked in pediatric oncology. We also reviewed ISMP-recommended outcome measures: drug library compliance rate, rate of soft-limit overrides, and the proportion of "saved" programming errors caught by hard limits.

What surprised me was learning that the pump library is only as safe as its ongoing maintenance. Pharmacy teams must regularly review override data and near-miss reports to refine concentrations, rates, and limits — otherwise the library drifts out of date and clinicians override it routinely. This reframed smart pump deployment from a one-time technology purchase to an ongoing pharmacy stewardship activity. I had not appreciated how much of medication safety technology depends on the pharmacist's analytical work behind the scenes.

I will apply this by (1) asking my hospital preceptor whether I can review the most recent smart pump override report for one care unit and identify any high-frequency overrides that suggest a library update is needed; (2) shadowing the medication safety pharmacist if the institution has one to learn how library changes are vetted and rolled out; and (3) committing to look at infusion pump technology not as a black box but as a pharmacy-curated safety asset that I will eventually be responsible for maintaining.

reflection_text:
This week's class focused on why pediatric patients are at disproportionately high risk for high-alert medication errors and the engineering, process, and communication safeguards specific to that population. I learned that nearly every dose in pediatrics is weight-based, that small math errors produce 10-fold or 100-fold dosing errors more often than in adults, and that concentration confusion (especially with morphine, fentanyl, and heparin) is a leading driver of pediatric HAM events. We also reviewed safeguards: standardized concentrations across the institution, pre-printed pediatric order sets that pre-calculate doses, mandatory weight verification in kg only (no lbs in the EHR), and dispensing in patient-specific rather than floor-stock form for the highest-risk products.

This learning changed my picture of pediatric pharmacy practice in a couple of ways. I had previously thought of pediatric dosing as primarily a calculation skill. After this class I understand it as a system-design problem in which the pharmacist's role is to remove opportunities for the calculation to be wrong — through standard concentrations, smart pump libraries with pediatric hard limits, and dispensing decisions that put the right dose in the right container. The personal piece for me was realizing that, in a pediatric rotation, my responsibility is not only to verify doses correctly but to advocate for upstream system fixes when I notice a recurring near-miss pattern.

I will apply this by (1) requesting a pediatric rotation week if my program permits, with the explicit goal of observing how pediatric HAM dispensing differs from adult; (2) asking my preceptor to walk me through one pediatric near-miss report and the system change (or non-change) it triggered; and (3) building a personal checklist for myself that I will use whenever I verify a pediatric HAM order — confirm weight in kg, confirm concentration matches institutional standard, confirm dose is within published pediatric range, and double-check the math against an independent reference.

reflection_text:
This session covered anticoagulation management at transitions of care, which the ISMP and Joint Commission identify as one of the highest-risk periods for HAM-related patient harm. I learned that errors cluster at three transitions: (1) hospital admission, where home anticoagulant regimens are not reconciled and patients receive duplicate therapy; (2) intra-hospital transitions (e.g., ICU to floor) where bridging plans are dropped or doses are restarted incorrectly after procedures; and (3) discharge, where DOACs are converted back from heparin without clear instructions and patients leave without filled prescriptions or follow-up. We reviewed evidence-based safeguards: a pharmacist-led anticoagulation reconciliation at each transition, a structured discharge counseling encounter that includes teach-back, and post-discharge follow-up calls within 48–72 hours.

What struck me was the data on how often anticoagulant transitions go wrong even at high-performing institutions, and how often the pharmacist is the only team member who can catch the error. I had previously thought of "medication reconciliation" as a generic checkbox activity. This session reframed it as the highest-leverage anticoagulation safety intervention available, and as an explicit pharmacy responsibility that I should pursue rather than wait to be asked about.

I plan to apply this by (1) asking my preceptor on an inpatient rotation if I can lead the anticoagulation reconciliation for at least two patients per shift, with their oversight; (2) shadowing a discharge counseling encounter for a newly anticoagulated patient and using teach-back myself before the rotation ends; and (3) reviewing the institution's anticoagulation transition protocol and identifying one gap I can write up for my preceptor as a learning exercise.

reflection_text:
Today's session focused on the opioid Patient-Controlled Analgesia (PCA) pump as one of the highest-risk routine HAM workflows in hospital pharmacy. I learned that PCA-related sentinel events typically cluster around three failure modes: programming errors at pump setup (concentration, bolus, lockout), "PCA by proxy" (a family member or even staff member presses the button on a sedated patient's behalf, defeating the safety logic of the patient-controlled design), and inadequate respiratory monitoring during the first 24 hours when opioid-naive patients are at highest risk for respiratory depression. The class reviewed the ISMP guidance: standardized PCA order sets that prevent free-text dosing, mandatory continuous capnography for all opioid-naive PCA patients, and explicit "no PCA by proxy" patient and family education.

This was important to me because opioid PCA had felt like a routine inpatient tool I would barely think about. The class made clear that the pump's safety hinges on a chain of small details — pharmacist verification of the concentration, nursing programming, monitoring escalation thresholds — any of which can fail silently. The PCA-by-proxy detail was the most striking; I had not realized that a well-meaning family member is one of the most common contributors to PCA harm events.

I will apply this by (1) reviewing the institution's PCA order set and policy on my first inpatient rotation and identifying any gaps relative to ISMP guidance; (2) sitting in on at least one PCA initiation with the primary nurse to observe the patient and family education in real time; and (3) when I verify a PCA order, deliberately checking opioid-naive status and confirming continuous capnography is ordered before signing off — this becomes a personal habit rather than an institutional rule.

reflection_text:
This session covered neuromuscular blocking agents (NMBs) — the paralytic drugs used during intubation and mechanical ventilation — as one of the most lethal medication-error categories because the harm pathway is rapid and the antidote is not always available. I learned that the canonical NMB error is inadvertent administration to a patient who is not mechanically ventilated, which produces respiratory arrest within minutes. ISMP has documented multiple deaths from rocuronium or vecuronium being mistakenly substituted for routine medications (Versed and others) due to look-alike vials, automated dispensing cabinet override use, and confusion in storage location. The recommended safeguards are: store NMBs only in locations where they will be used (OR, ICU, ED), use red-bordered "WARNING: PARALYZING AGENT" auxiliary labels, and remove NMBs from automated cabinet override lists.

What this changed for me was a sense of which HAMs sit at the top of the harm severity scale. Insulin and heparin produce more total errors but allow time to intervene; an NMB error in an unventilated patient is functionally irreversible. That severity asymmetry justifies the very specific storage and labeling controls — they are about preventing the small number of events that you cannot recover from.

I will apply this by (1) on my first ICU or ED rotation, asking how NMBs are stored and confirming the auxiliary labels are present; (2) reviewing the automated dispensing cabinet override list at the institution and checking that NMBs are removed; and (3) committing to push back firmly if I am ever asked to dispense an NMB to a unit that does not routinely manage ventilated patients — that is a stop-the-line moment.

reflection_text:
Today's session focused on concentrated electrolytes — particularly potassium chloride (KCl), hypertonic saline (3%, 23.4%), and concentrated magnesium sulfate — as a HAM category that ISMP has issued specific structural guidance on. I learned that the canonical error is administering concentrated KCl undiluted, which produces fatal cardiac arrhythmia within minutes. The Joint Commission's 1998 Sentinel Event Alert effectively removed concentrated KCl from most floor stock nationwide. We reviewed the current ISMP guidance: store concentrated electrolytes only in the pharmacy or specifically designated areas (ICU, OR), dispense only as patient-specific premixed bags with standardized concentrations, and require prescriber justification for any non-standard concentration.

What this was important for was recognizing that concentrated electrolyte policy is one of the clearest examples of system-level safety design actually working — sentinel events have decreased substantially since the 1998 alert. The personal piece was understanding that the policy depends on no single pharmacist deciding to override it 'just this once,' because the exception cases compound across the system. I had previously thought of floor stock decisions as logistical; the class reframed them as safety engineering.

I will apply this by (1) on my first inpatient rotation, asking where concentrated KCl and hypertonic saline are stored and confirming they are not in general floor stock; (2) reviewing the institution's premix concentration list and noting which non-standard requests triggered exception approvals in the past year; and (3) committing to never approve an undiluted concentrated electrolyte for ward administration without a documented exception process — even if the request comes from a senior physician.

reflection_text:
This session focused on methotrexate dosing errors, specifically the frequency confusion between weekly low-dose methotrexate (used for rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis, and ectopic pregnancy management) and the daily dosing common in oncology and trophoblastic disease protocols. I learned that the canonical error is a patient taking a weekly methotrexate dose every day — typically because the prescription was written without an explicit "weekly" indication, the pharmacy label said "take as directed," or the patient misunderstood the schedule. The harm pathway is bone marrow suppression, mucositis, and death within days; ISMP has documented dozens of fatalities from this error pattern. Recommended safeguards include: requiring the day of the week and explicit "weekly" wording on the label, requiring indication on every methotrexate prescription, and using teach-back at every dispensing encounter.

This was important to me because methotrexate is widely dispensed in community pharmacy and the error is invisible at the dispensing window — the prescription itself often looks correct, and the failure happens days later in the patient's home. I had been thinking of HAM safety as primarily an inpatient concern; this session reframed methotrexate as the most consequential HAM I will encounter in retail practice. The personal piece was a heightened sense of how much patient counseling really matters for this drug — it is the only safety net once the bottle leaves the counter.

I will apply this by (1) committing, at every methotrexate dispensing on my retail rotation, to use teach-back rather than the standard "any questions?" close; (2) flagging any methotrexate prescription without an explicit weekly day to my preceptor for prescriber clarification; and (3) reviewing the institutional or chain auxiliary label policy for methotrexate and proposing improvements if the language is not explicit.

reflection_text:
This session covered vasopressors and emergency medications — norepinephrine, epinephrine, vasopressin, dopamine — as HAMs whose errors typically occur under time pressure during code response or rapid clinical deterioration. I learned that the high-risk error patterns include concentration confusion (epinephrine 1:1,000 IM/SC vs 1:10,000 IV), rapid-bolus administration of drugs intended only for infusion, and dose-doubling when verbal orders are called during a code without standardized closed-loop communication. We reviewed the safeguards: standardized concentrations in the institution's code carts, removing the 1:1000 ratio expression in favor of mg/mL labeling, and TeamSTEPPS-style closed-loop confirmation during verbal orders.

This shifted how I think about emergency medications. I had imagined that code response is mostly muscle memory and protocol-driven — and it is — but that is precisely why subtle concentration or labeling differences cause harm. People follow the rehearsed pattern; if the labeling departs from the pattern, the wrong action gets executed at speed. The personal piece was appreciating how much the pharmacy team's choices about code-cart concentrations and labeling shape what happens in a code six hours later, even though no one in the code remembers it was a pharmacy decision.

I will apply this by (1) on my hospital rotation, requesting to inspect a code cart and confirm the vasopressor labeling matches ISMP's mg/mL guidance; (2) attending one mock code if my preceptor permits, specifically to observe how verbal medication orders are read back; and (3) when I encounter a non-standard concentration request from a code team during my career, escalating it as a system question rather than treating it as a one-off.

reflection_text:
This session focused on oral chemotherapy administered at home — capecitabine, imatinib, lenalidomide, and similar agents — as a category that combines HAM risk with the absence of inpatient safeguards. I learned that oral chemo shifts the safety burden to the patient, the caregiver, and the dispensing pharmacist (often retail) who has neither the training nor the workflow that an inpatient oncology pharmacy provides. Common errors include patients missing doses or doubling up, mishandling of cytotoxic waste at home, dose modifications not communicated by the oncology clinic to the dispensing pharmacy, and drug interactions with patients' other home medications. ASCO and HOPA have published explicit oral chemotherapy safety standards, including pharmacist-led dispensing protocols, structured patient education, and proactive adherence monitoring.

What this was important for was recognizing that oral chemotherapy is an increasingly common dispensing scenario that most pharmacists are not explicitly trained for. The personal piece was realizing that retail pharmacists are now part of the oncology safety chain whether they recognize it or not, and that the right response is to upskill rather than to defer to the prescribing clinic.

I will apply this by (1) on my retail rotation, reviewing the chain or site's oral chemotherapy dispensing protocol if one exists, and asking what training is required; (2) completing the HOPA oral chemotherapy safety modules before I am responsible for these dispenses independently; and (3) for at least one oral chemo dispense, doing structured patient education with handout, teach-back, and a follow-up call within the first week.

Today's session covered tall-man lettering and the broader naming-standards approach to LASA error reduction. I learned that tall-man lettering — writing parts of confusable drug names in capital letters (e.g., hydrOXYzine vs hydrALAZINE, vinBLAStine vs vinCRIStine) — is recommended by ISMP and FDA as one of the few proven label-level interventions for LASA errors. We reviewed the published lists (ISMP confused-name pairs, FDA's Name Differentiation Project), as well as the limitations: tall-man lettering works only when it appears consistently across the EHR, dispensing label, and stocking shelf, and only when prescribers also use it. We also covered broader naming safeguards including indication-required prescribing, look-alike storage separation, and the use of generic-only or brand-only labels in high-risk categories.

This learning gave me a clearer picture of why some institutions still experience LASA errors despite having tall-man lettering on dispensing labels — the labels alone are not enough if the EHR order screens, the shelf labels, and the prescriber's e-prescribing software don't carry the same convention. That points toward a system-level question rather than a labeling fix. The personal piece for me was appreciating that tall-man lettering is a tool whose effectiveness depends on consistency across an ecosystem that no single pharmacist controls.

I will apply this by (1) reviewing the ISMP and FDA tall-man lettering lists before my retail rotation so I can spot inconsistencies in real workflow; (2) checking, on my first hospital shift, whether tall-man lettering appears consistently in EHR, dispensing label, and shelf label and reporting any inconsistencies to my preceptor; and (3) committing to use tall-man lettering in any clinical note, handoff, or email I write professionally — the convention only works if everyone uses it.

reflection_text:
This session focused on bedside Bar Code Medication Administration (BCMA) as the technological control most closely associated with reductions in wrong-drug and wrong-patient HAM errors. I learned that BCMA works by scanning the patient's wristband and the medication packaging at the bedside, with the EHR confirming match against the active medication order before administration. We reviewed published data showing 50%+ reductions in wrong-drug errors at hospitals that implement BCMA with high compliance — and equally important, the workarounds that reduce that benefit: scanning a printed copy of the wristband instead of the patient's actual wristband, scanning the medication before drawing it up, and the cultural pattern in which override is treated as routine. ISMP guidance is to make compliance visible, audit overrides, and design the workflow so that scanning is the fastest path rather than the slower one.

What this was important for was understanding the conditional nature of technology safety controls. BCMA works in environments where the workflow and culture support it; in environments where overrides are normalized, the data shows compliance numbers that look healthy but harm rates that don't improve. The personal piece for me was realizing that, when I am eventually involved in implementing or improving BCMA-related processes, the question isn't whether the technology is deployed but whether the workflow makes the right behavior easier than the workaround.

I will apply this by (1) shadowing one nursing shift on my hospital rotation to observe BCMA scanning in practice, with attention to overrides and workarounds; (2) asking the medication safety officer for the institution's most recent BCMA compliance report and override-pattern analysis; and (3) committing, when I see a BCMA workaround in real time, to debrief the moment with the nurse rather than report it from a distance — that is the way frontline behavior actually changes.

reflection_text: In today's session, we did a deep dive into how the IHI Model for Improvement, and specifically the Plan-Do-Study-Act cycle, can be applied to the problem of medication reconciliation errors at care transitions. We learned that medication reconciliation, the process of comparing a patient's medication orders to all of the medications the patient has been taking, is one of the most error-prone steps in the care continuum, particularly at hospital admission, transfer, and discharge. The IHI framework begins with three foundational questions before any PDSA cycle is run: What are we trying to accomplish? How will we know that a change is an improvement? What changes can we make that will result in improvement? We worked through a case study in which a hospital pharmacy team used PDSA cycles to test a pharmacist-led medication history intake process at admission, measuring discrepancy rates before and after the intervention. The first cycle revealed that the new form was too long for patients with multiple chronic conditions, so the team adapted the form in the second cycle and saw a measurable reduction in undocumented discrepancies. This session was transformative for my professional development because it connected a quality improvement framework I had previously thought of as administrative to a concrete, high-stakes clinical problem I will face as a pharmacist. Medication reconciliation errors cause real patient harm, including duplicate therapies, missed doses of critical medications, and dangerous drug interactions that go undetected because the full medication picture was never assembled correctly. Understanding that I can use PDSA cycles to systematically test and refine my own reconciliation process, rather than hoping I get it right through experience alone, gives me a concrete method for improving over time. I also realized that pharmacists are uniquely positioned to lead reconciliation improvement projects because of our medication expertise and our role across all care settings. I will apply this by designing a small PDSA cycle at my internship site focused on medication reconciliation at patient discharge. Specifically, I will plan a structured reconciliation checklist to use during discharge counseling, do it with a defined set of patients over two weeks, study whether it reduces the number of discrepancies identified at the follow-up call, and act by refining the checklist based on what I learned before expanding use.

reflection_text: This session focused on two foundational components of the IHI Model for Improvement that are often underappreciated: writing effective aim statements and selecting meaningful measures of improvement. We learned that a well-constructed aim statement answers three specific questions: what exactly will be improved, by how much, and by when. Vague aim statements like 'improve patient safety' are not actionable and make it impossible to evaluate whether a change actually worked. We reviewed examples such as 'reduce the rate of dispensing errors caught at the final verification step by 25 percent within 90 days' as a strong aim versus 'reduce errors in the pharmacy' as a weak one. We also learned that the IHI framework distinguishes between three types of measures: outcome measures, which track the result you ultimately care about for patients; process measures, which track whether the intervention is actually being performed as intended; and balancing measures, which track whether fixing one part of the system creates a problem elsewhere. For instance, if a pharmacy implements a new barcode verification step to reduce dispensing errors, the outcome measure might be the dispensing error rate, the process measure might be the percentage of prescriptions scanned with the barcode, and the balancing measure might be the average fill time to ensure the new step does not create unacceptable delays. This session significantly advanced my understanding of quality improvement because I had previously thought of measurement as a bureaucratic requirement, but now I see it as the mechanism through which you know whether your effort actually helped. Without clear measures, a team can implement changes indefinitely and never know if patient outcomes improved. For pharmacists, this is especially important because we are often asked to participate in quality improvement committees and pharmacy and therapeutics reviews, and I will need to be able to evaluate whether proposed changes are backed by well-designed measurement. I will apply this by ensuring that any PDSA project I propose or contribute to during my internship includes all three measure types from the start. I will also practice writing aim statements using the IHI structure and ask my preceptor to critique them before I begin any small-scale test of change.

reflection_text: In today's class we examined one of the more advanced but critically important aspects of the IHI Model for Improvement: spreading and sustaining changes that have been validated through PDSA cycles. We learned that the IHI framework treats spread not as an automatic step after a successful test, but as a deliberate process that requires its own planning. Key principles of spread include identifying and engaging the populations and settings where the change will be implemented, ensuring that the people who will carry out the change understand the evidence behind it, adapting the change for new contexts without losing its essential components, and building in ongoing measurement to detect whether the change remains effective over time. We reviewed a case study of a community pharmacy chain that successfully used PDSA cycles to reduce patient wait times at one high-volume location and then attempted to spread the intervention to ten other sites. The spread effort partially failed because the team assumed that what worked in one location would work identically in others without adapting to differences in staffing models and patient volume patterns. This was a pivotal insight: a validated improvement is only as durable as the system it is embedded in, and spread requires the same iterative thinking as initial testing. This learning matters enormously to my development as a pharmacist because it dismantles a naive assumption I had held, which was that if something works once, you simply repeat it. Real quality improvement requires attention to context, adaptation, and sustained monitoring. As someone who may eventually move between practice sites or take on leadership roles, I need to understand how to transfer improvements across settings rather than starting from scratch each time. I will apply this at my internship by asking my preceptor to show me examples of quality improvement initiatives that were spread across the practice site and discussing what adaptations were made between the pilot and the full rollout. I will also pay attention to whether any implemented changes are still being actively monitored or whether measurement stopped after the initial improvement was declared.

reflection_text: This session applied the IHI Model for Improvement specifically to the domain of patient medication counseling and education, an area where pharmacists have a direct and measurable impact on patient outcomes. We learned that the quality of medication counseling, including whether patients actually understand their dosing schedule, potential side effects, and what to do if they miss a dose, is highly variable and rarely systematically measured. Using the three IHI questions as a framework, we workshopped how a pharmacy team might structure a quality improvement initiative around counseling: the aim could be to increase the proportion of patients who can correctly recall the purpose and dosing schedule of a newly dispensed medication immediately after counseling from a baseline of 40 percent to 75 percent within 60 days. We then walked through how PDSA cycles could test different counseling approaches, such as a structured teach-back method or a simplified medication information card, measuring recall rates after each cycle before scaling up the most effective approach. We also discussed how the IHI model could be used to address health literacy disparities, by testing counseling adaptations specifically designed for patients with low literacy or language barriers and measuring whether those adaptations narrow the gap in recall rates. This session deepened my understanding of the pharmacist's clinical role because it showed me that counseling is not simply an interpersonal skill that improves with experience. It is a process that can be designed, measured, and systematically improved using the same quality improvement tools used in high-stakes medication safety interventions. I had previously thought of counseling as something I would get better at intuitively over time, but this session challenged me to think about how I can evaluate whether patients are actually retaining the information I provide, and to adapt my approach based on evidence rather than comfort. I will apply this by designing a small PDSA project during my internship focused on new prescription counseling. I will plan a structured teach-back approach for one class of medications, such as anticoagulants, do it consistently over two weeks, study whether patients at their follow-up contact or callback can correctly answer two key questions about their medication, and act by refining the counseling script or tools based on what I observe. I will share my findings with my preceptor and ask whether the method could be adopted more broadly at the site.

reflection_text:
Today's session covered run charts and statistical process control (SPC) charts as the IHI Model for Improvement's core data display tools. I learned that a run chart plots a measure over time on the y-axis and uses three statistical rules — shift (six or more consecutive points on one side of the median), trend (five or more points consistently up or down), and astronomical points — to distinguish signal from noise. We also covered when to upgrade to a control chart (typically once you have ~20–25 baseline data points), and the difference between common-cause variation (inherent in the system) and special-cause variation (a specific assignable event). The instructor walked us through a case where a hospital celebrated a downward trend in central line infections that was actually within normal variation — a costly misinterpretation that a control chart would have caught.

This learning was important because I previously assumed that a few consecutive months of improvement meant a change had worked. The class clarified that without statistical structure, improvement teams can chase noise, declare premature victory, or abandon a working change because of one bad month. For pharmacy specifically, where measures like medication error rates have low base rates and high variability, this is the difference between a real improvement and a story.

I will apply this by (1) building a run chart for one process at my internship — for example, the daily count of dispensing errors caught at verification — using ten weeks of historical data and applying the three rules; (2) asking my preceptor whether the institution uses control charts in any ongoing QI projects and if so, requesting permission to view one; and (3) taking the IHI Open School module on run charts before my next QI rotation so I can read and critique a chart fluently rather than just nod along.
