reflection_text:
This week's session introduced driver diagrams, the IHI tool used to translate an aim statement into a visible theory of change. A driver diagram has three tiers: the aim (what we will improve, by how much, by when), the primary drivers (the high-level factors believed to influence the aim), and the secondary drivers and change ideas (specific testable actions). We worked through a community pharmacy example aimed at improving pneumococcal vaccination rates among adults ≥65, mapping primary drivers (patient awareness, pharmacist workflow, payer logistics) to specific change ideas (point-of-sale prompts, standing orders, bundled flu/pneumonia outreach calls). The instructor emphasized that the diagram is a hypothesis, not a permanent plan — change ideas that don't move the measure get pruned.

What this learning gave me was a way to defend or challenge a QI project before any PDSA cycle is run. Before today, I would have started a project by picking a change idea that seemed reasonable. The driver diagram forces the team to articulate why the change idea is expected to move the measure — and to surface assumptions early. For me personally, it also clarified why some QI projects I have observed felt scattershot: the team had no shared theory of change, just a list of activities.

I plan to apply this by (1) building my own driver diagram for one workflow problem at my internship (e.g., naloxone dispensing rate to opioid prescription fills) before I propose any change idea to my preceptor; (2) asking to review an existing institutional driver diagram on a current QI project to see how a mature team has structured one; and (3) using the diagram explicitly when I discuss QI ideas with my preceptor so we are debating the underlying theory and not just the activities.

reflection_text:
This session focused on the human side of the IHI Model for Improvement: engaging frontline staff in change. I learned that the most common reason PDSA cycles fail is not technical but social — when frontline pharmacists, technicians, and nurses are not part of designing the change, they don't execute it during the test, and the resulting data tells you nothing about the change idea. We reviewed evidence-based engagement strategies: identifying informal influencers on a unit before launching a test, running early PDSA cycles with the most willing volunteers (not the highest-volume area), and using brief stand-up debriefs at the end of each shift to capture what frontline staff observed.

This was important to me because I had been thinking of QI primarily as a technical exercise — choose a measure, run a PDSA, look at the data. The session reframed it as a leadership exercise in which the data is only trustworthy if the people generating it are bought in. It also resonated with experiences I have had on rotation watching technicians be told to follow a new workflow without being told why, and seeing the workflow drift back within two weeks.

I will apply this by (1) committing, on any QI project I am part of, to interview at least two frontline staff members before proposing a change idea, with the explicit purpose of learning what they think would work and what won't; (2) building a debrief into any test cycle I run on rotation, even if it's just a five-minute end-of-shift huddle; and (3) keeping a personal log of which engagement strategies seem to work in different unit cultures so I am building a body of practical knowledge over the course of my rotations.

reflection_text:
This session introduced Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA) as a proactive companion to the IHI Model for Improvement. Where PDSA cycles test a change to make a system better, FMEA examines a process to predict where it could fail before harm occurs. I learned the structured FMEA steps: map the process, identify potential failure modes at each step, score each on likelihood, severity, and detectability to produce a Risk Priority Number (RPN), and prioritize redesign on the highest-RPN failure modes. We worked through an FMEA on a hospital pharmacy chemotherapy compounding workflow and the highest-RPN failure mode turned out to be an early step (label generation) that the team had not previously considered risky.

What struck me about FMEA is how often the most dangerous failure mode is not the one that has caused harm yet. PDSA improves what you can already measure; FMEA forces you to look at what hasn't broken yet. As a future pharmacist, this matters because high-alert medication processes — chemotherapy, anticoagulation, TPN — are exactly the kind of low-frequency, high-severity systems where waiting for an event to drive learning is too costly.

I plan to apply this by (1) asking my preceptor whether the institution has run an FMEA on any pharmacy process in the last two years and, if so, requesting to see the worksheet so I can study the structure; (2) running my own informal FMEA on one workflow at my internship (e.g., the e-prescription verification process for controlled substances) and bringing the analysis to my preceptor for critique; and (3) committing, when I reach independent practice, to use FMEA before launching any new HAM-related workflow rather than relying on PDSA after the fact.

reflection_text:
This session focused specifically on balancing measures within the IHI Model for Improvement — the third leg of the family of measures (alongside outcome and process) that tracks what is getting WORSE while you are trying to make something better. I learned the canonical examples: a hospital running a PDSA cycle to reduce ED door-to-doctor time may inadvertently increase readmission rates if the time pressure compresses workup; a community pharmacy improving immunization rate may inadvertently extend wait times for routine prescriptions, reducing patient satisfaction and long-term retention. The IHI principle is that every change has a balancing measure, and an improvement project that doesn't define one is hiding from its own consequences.

What this clarified for me was a recurring pattern I had observed without naming: improvement projects that celebrate a primary measure while quietly producing problems on adjacent measures. The class reframed balancing measures as not optional documentation but a moral commitment — you are looking for the harm your intervention might cause, in advance, and tracking it. The personal piece was realizing that as a future pharmacist-improver, my credibility on a project depends on whether I have named my balancing measures, not just my aim measure.

I will apply this by (1) committing, on any QI project I am part of, to explicitly name at least one balancing measure during the design phase, not during the analysis; (2) asking my preceptor to share one institutional improvement project where a balancing measure caught a problem and the team adjusted; and (3) when I review someone else's QI proposal, specifically asking what's being tracked that could go wrong — that is the highest-leverage feedback I can give.

reflection_text:
Today's class introduced Lean methodology — adapted from the Toyota Production System — as it has been applied to healthcare improvement, alongside the IHI Model for Improvement. I learned the core Lean concepts: identifying the eight wastes (Defects, Overproduction, Waiting, Non-utilized talent, Transportation, Inventory, Motion, Extra processing — DOWNTIME), value-stream mapping to make the current state visible, and continuous flow with pull (rather than push) production. We reviewed pharmacy applications: redesigning the dispensing workflow to reduce technician motion and waiting, using kanban systems for inventory management, and value-stream mapping the discharge medication reconciliation process. The instructor emphasized that Lean and IHI are complementary — Lean is strong at process redesign and waste elimination; IHI is strong at testing changes and learning from data.

What this was important for was a more pluralistic view of improvement work. I had been thinking of IHI's Model for Improvement as the framework. The class gave me a richer toolkit, and the value-stream mapping exercise in particular made visible how much of pharmacy workflow is invisible motion and waiting that no one designs intentionally — it just accumulates. Realizing that meant I now look at workflow with a different eye.

I will apply this by (1) value-stream mapping one workflow at my internship — for example, the e-prescription verification process — using the Lean DOWNTIME categories to identify waste; (2) reading one chapter of Mark Graban's Lean Hospitals before my next QI rotation to deepen the vocabulary; and (3) when I encounter a workflow problem, asking first "what kind of waste is this?" before proposing a change idea.

reflection_text:
This session covered Six Sigma and the DMAIC framework (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) as another structured approach to quality improvement that intersects with the IHI Model for Improvement. I learned that Six Sigma originated in manufacturing and emphasizes statistical rigor in defect reduction — its name refers to a defect rate of 3.4 per million opportunities, achieved through process control. In healthcare, full Six Sigma implementations are less common than IHI/Lean hybrids, but the DMAIC framework is widely adapted: Define the problem precisely, Measure baseline performance, Analyze causes (often using cause-and-effect / Ishikawa diagrams), Improve with tested interventions, and Control with ongoing monitoring. The class also introduced Lean Six Sigma as the hybrid most commonly applied to pharmacy and hospital operations.

What this was important for was understanding that the improvement field has multiple coexisting frameworks and that, in practice, hospitals often blend them. The DMAIC structure gave me a clearer mental map than I had before — particularly the distinction between Improve (testing) and Control (sustaining). The personal piece was realizing that I will likely encounter all three vocabularies (IHI, Lean, Six Sigma) in different institutions and should be fluent in each rather than partisan about one.

I plan to apply this by (1) earning at least the Six Sigma Yellow Belt credential during my advanced rotations or first job, since it requires only modest time investment and is widely recognized; (2) on any QI project I join, asking which framework the team is using and whether that choice matches the problem; and (3) using the DMAIC structure as a personal discipline when I am drafting an improvement proposal — even if the team calls the work "PDSA," running my proposal through DMAIC as a check.

reflection_text:
Today's session was a case-based application of the IHI Model for Improvement to an adverse drug event (ADE) reduction project at an academic medical center. The project began with an aim statement: reduce inpatient ADE rate (per 1,000 patient-days) by 25% within 12 months. The team's driver diagram identified primary drivers (high-risk medication monitoring, transitions of care, and prescribing decision support) and tested change ideas through PDSA cycles: pharmacist-led dose-adjustment for renal function, automated alerts for QT-prolonging combinations, and a structured discharge medication reconciliation. We reviewed the run charts at six and twelve months — a clear shift below the median for the QT alert intervention, and a non-significant trend for the discharge reconciliation that the team is now redesigning.

What this case clarified was how the IHI tools we have studied separately actually fit together in a real project — the aim, the driver diagram, the PDSA cycles, the run chart, and the iteration. I had been studying each tool somewhat in isolation. Seeing the whole arc of a project made visible how the tools cohere, and also how often the first change ideas don't move the measure. The personal piece was a realistic sense of the tempo of improvement work — twelve months for a 25% target is fast, not slow, and a non-significant result at twelve months is a reason to redesign, not abandon.

I will apply this by (1) asking my preceptor whether I can review one completed institutional ADE-reduction project and study the run charts, PDSA logs, and final report; (2) building an ADE reduction project of my own scope at my retail rotation — for example, reducing NSAID-warfarin co-prescriptions through pharmacist outreach — to practice the full IHI arc; and (3) accepting upfront that the first PDSA cycle on any of my future projects is unlikely to move the measure, so I will plan three cycles before evaluating my approach.

reflection_text:
This session covered 30-day hospital readmission reduction as a high-stakes IHI improvement application — high-stakes because CMS penalizes hospitals with excessive readmissions through the Hospital Readmissions Reduction Program (HRRP). I learned that medication-related causes contribute to roughly 20% of preventable readmissions and that pharmacist-led interventions — discharge medication reconciliation, post-discharge follow-up calls, and meds-to-beds programs — have published evidence of reducing readmissions in heart failure, COPD, and AMI populations. The IHI framing for this work involves a clear aim (reduce 30-day readmission for the target population by X% in Y months), explicit balancing measures (LOS, ED utilization, patient satisfaction), and PDSA cycles testing one element at a time before bundling.

What this clarified for me was the alignment of clinical pharmacy practice with health-system financial incentives. I had been thinking of clinical pharmacy services as good practice that hospitals should fund. The class reframed transitions-of-care pharmacy as an HRRP-aligned investment that has direct ROI to the institution — which is the argument that gets services funded. The personal piece was a clearer sense of how to make the business case for clinical pharmacy roles when I am eventually advocating for one.

I will apply this by (1) asking my hospital preceptor whether the institution has a transitions-of-care pharmacist role and, if so, requesting to shadow for one shift; (2) reviewing the CMS HRRP rules so I understand the financial pressure the institution is responding to; and (3) building, before I graduate, a written elevator pitch for the value of pharmacist-led transitions-of-care services that I can use in interviews and committee meetings.

reflection_text:
Today's session applied the IHI Model for Improvement to antimicrobial stewardship — the practice of optimizing antibiotic use to reduce resistance, C. difficile infections, and avoidable adverse drug reactions. I learned that effective stewardship programs have measurable aims (e.g., reduce DOT — days of therapy — per 1,000 patient-days by 15% over two years), use a family of measures including process measures (proportion of antibiotic orders meeting institutional criteria), outcome measures (C. diff incidence, resistance trends), and balancing measures (treatment failures, length of stay), and cycle through PDSA tests of specific interventions: prospective audit and feedback, prior authorization for broad-spectrum agents, automatic IV-to-PO conversion, and pharmacist-driven renal dose adjustment.

What this was important for was seeing antimicrobial stewardship as an explicit improvement program rather than as a clinical service that exists by tradition. The IHI framing made clear why some stewardship programs are successful and others stall — the successful ones operate as continuous improvement projects with measurable aims and clear balancing measures, while the stalled ones run on physician charisma and quarterly memos. The personal piece was understanding that stewardship is one of the most evidence-rich improvement areas in pharmacy and probably the easiest to use as a portfolio project for a new pharmacist building credibility.

I will apply this by (1) requesting an infectious-disease or stewardship rotation if my program permits, with a focus on the QI infrastructure rather than just the clinical content; (2) reviewing one institutional stewardship report and identifying which IHI tools were used; and (3) when I am eventually a hospital staff pharmacist, volunteering to run one stewardship-related PDSA cycle in my first six months — that is the fastest visible-improvement project available to a junior pharmacist.

reflection_text:
This session focused on diabetes management improvement in ambulatory settings — specifically increasing the proportion of patients with type 2 diabetes meeting A1C targets — as an example of how the IHI Model for Improvement applies to community pharmacy and primary care. I learned that the most successful programs combine pharmacist medication therapy management (MTM), structured patient education, and continuous glucose monitoring access, and use a clear aim (e.g., increase the proportion of panel patients with A1C < 8% from 65% to 80% in 12 months). The driver diagram typically includes primary drivers around medication optimization, self-management education, and care coordination, with secondary drivers down to specific PDSA-testable change ideas (a 10-minute pharmacist visit at month 1, 3, and 6; standardized teach-back on insulin technique; an automated panel report flagging out-of-target patients).

What this was important for was recognizing that ambulatory pharmacy is one of the most fertile places for QI work because the patient population is stable enough to track outcomes over months. Inpatient improvement projects are constrained by short LOS; ambulatory diabetes work can show real A1C movement. The personal piece was realizing that I should pursue an ambulatory rotation specifically with the goal of running a small QI project, because the time horizon is favorable and the data infrastructure (panels, A1C results) is readily available.

I will apply this by (1) requesting a longitudinal ambulatory rotation if my program permits, with a written QI project as the deliverable; (2) before the rotation, reading the IHI A1C improvement white paper to calibrate my expectations of pace and impact; and (3) committing to the discipline of A1C as a true outcome measure rather than allowing my project to drift into process-only metrics that look successful but don't move patient health.

reflection_text:
Today's final IHI session focused on a community-pharmacy QI project I will almost certainly encounter or run within my first two years of practice: improving adult vaccination rates (pneumococcal, shingles, RSV, influenza). I learned that the highest-leverage change idea is point-of-sale prompts tied to refill events — for every prescription pickup, the system flags vaccines the patient is eligible for but has not received. Combined with pharmacist-initiated standing orders and brief teach-back on vaccine rationale, published programs have shown adult vaccination rates increase from baseline 30-40% to 60%+ within 18 months. The IHI framing here is particularly clean: a measurable aim, a clear driver diagram (awareness, convenience, payer coverage), PDSA-testable change ideas, and balancing measures (waiting times, patient satisfaction).

What this was important for was seeing how community pharmacy QI can be a career-building activity rather than just an operational duty. A documented, data-driven vaccination improvement project is a portfolio piece that translates into residency applications, advanced practice opportunities, and management roles. The personal piece was clarifying that I should begin tracking my QI work formally from the start of my first job — even small projects, documented well, compound into a body of work.

I will apply this by (1) on my retail rotation, identifying the site's current adult vaccination rate and proposing one PDSA test (e.g., a refill prompt) to my preceptor; (2) keeping a personal QI portfolio document starting in my P4 year that captures every project I run with aim, driver diagram, and outcome data; and (3) committing, when I eventually lead a vaccination project, to publish the results in a state pharmacy journal — sharing the data publicly raises the standard of practice.

reflection_text: In today's class, we covered the concept of Just Culture and how it shapes the way organizations respond to medication errors. We reviewed the three behaviors in the Just Culture model: human error, which calls for consoling the person involved; at-risk behavior, which calls for coaching; and reckless behavior, which calls for discipline. We also discussed how a traditional blame-based culture drives near misses underground and prevents healthcare teams from learning about the system weaknesses that those near misses reveal. Learning about Just Culture is essential for my development as a pharmacist because it challenged my assumption that every medication error should be treated the same way. I used to believe that any error meant someone was careless, but I now see that treating a harried technician who made an honest slip the same as someone who deliberately bypassed a verification step is both unjust and bad for patient safety. If the staff fears retaliation, errors go unreported, which means the underlying system problems stay invisible. At my internship, I will commit to filing a formal near miss report through our incident reporting system any time I catch an error before it reaches the patient, even when it feels minor. I will also ask my preceptor during our weekly check-in to walk through one recent reported event with me so that I can learn how our site distinguishes between human error, at-risk behavior, and reckless behavior in practice.

reflection_text: Today's session focused on Root Cause Analysis (RCA) as a structured method for investigating medication errors and adverse events. We walked through a case involving an insulin overdose and used the Five Whys technique to move past the initial human error down to the system-level contributors, including a look-alike vial design, inadequate labeling on the floor stock, and a nursing workflow that allowed the barcode scanner to be bypassed. We also discussed how a good RCA focuses on what happened and why, not on who was responsible, and how it should produce system-level action items rather than individual blame. Learning about RCA is essential to my growth because I previously thought of error investigations as fact-finding missions aimed at identifying who was at fault. This session reshaped my understanding by showing that stopping at 'the nurse drew up the wrong dose' prevents meaningful improvement, because the real fix lies upstream in packaging, storage, and workflow design. It also reinforced my belief that pharmacists have a unique responsibility to advocate for system changes, since we see the medication use process end-to-end from prescribing to administration. At my internship, I will ask my preceptor whether I can sit in on any RCA or M&M conferences that happen during my rotation, and if I catch a near miss at the counter I will document the contributing system factors such as storage layout, look-alike packaging, and interruption patterns, in addition to noting what the individual did.

reflection_text: In today's introduction to patient safety, we reviewed the key taxonomy used to classify safety events: near misses (errors that are caught before reaching the patient), medication errors that reach the patient without causing harm, adverse drug events (injury from a medication, whether preventable or not), and sentinel events (unexpected events that result in death or serious physical or psychological harm). We also discussed The Joint Commission's requirement that sentinel events trigger a formal review, and we mapped a sterile compounding case onto this taxonomy to see how the same underlying failure can fall into different categories depending on whether and how it reaches the patient. Learning this taxonomy is important for my growth because I had previously been using 'medication error' as a catch-all. Having precise language matters both for accurate documentation and for picking the right improvement response. It also challenged my assumption that near misses are 'not a big deal' because no patient was harmed. Near misses are actually the highest-volume, lowest-cost signal we have about system weaknesses, and they deserve the same scrutiny as events that caused harm. During my upcoming hospital rotation, I will ask my preceptor to walk me through how events are categorized in the hospital's reporting platform, and I will use the correct terminology in my own documentation instead of defaulting to 'error.' When I catch a near miss at work, I will file it through the same reporting pathway I would use for a harm event.

reflection_text: This session introduced human factors engineering and how cognitive errors contribute to medication safety events. We reviewed the main categories of cognitive failures, including slips (carrying out an intended action incorrectly, such as grabbing the wrong vial off the shelf), lapses (forgetting a step in a procedure), and mistakes (choosing the wrong course of action due to faulty reasoning or incomplete information). We also examined how confirmation bias, fatigue, interruptions, and high workload amplify these cognitive vulnerabilities, and how well-designed systems anticipate human error through forcing functions, redundancies, and standardization. A key takeaway was that human error is predictable and patterned, not a character flaw. This learning is foundational for my development as a pharmacist because it reframes errors as predictable responses to system conditions rather than moral failings. I reflected on times when I have almost made a dispensing error during busy shifts at my internship, and I realized that labeling myself 'careless' in those moments was both inaccurate and unhelpful. Recognizing workload, interruptions, and fatigue as measurable risk factors empowers me to advocate for workflow changes instead of relying on willpower to avoid mistakes. At my internship, I will start keeping a simple log of the situations in which I almost made an error (for example, when an interruption occurs mid-verification, or when I am the sole pharmacist on a holiday shift) so that I can bring concrete data to a conversation with my manager about workflow adjustments. I will also practice teach-back with patients at counseling so that my final cognitive check on a prescription happens in dialogue with the patient rather than alone in my head.

reflection_text:
Today's class introduced James Reason's Swiss Cheese Model as the foundational systems-thinking framework for patient safety. I learned that the model represents an organization's defenses as successive slices of cheese, each with holes (latent and active failures), and that a harm event occurs only when the holes momentarily align. The key insight is that no single slice (prescriber, pharmacist, nurse, technology) can be relied on to stop every error; instead, safety comes from layering imperfect defenses so that the holes rarely line up. We also reviewed Reason's distinction between active failures (unsafe acts at the sharp end — e.g., grabbing the wrong vial) and latent conditions (organizational decisions whose consequences lie dormant — understaffing, ambiguous policies, poor lighting). The class emphasized that addressing only active failures ("the technician was distracted") leaves the latent conditions that produced the distraction unchanged.

This shifted my mental model of safety in an important way. I had been instinctively treating individual errors as individual problems, especially in stories about medication errors that named the person involved. The Swiss Cheese Model reframes the same event as a system that allowed the error to reach the patient — and reframes the goal of safety work as adding or repairing defensive layers, not punishing individuals at the sharp end.

I will apply this in my rotations by (1) committing, when I encounter a near miss, to ask "what other defenses should have caught this?" before asking "who made the mistake?"; (2) asking my preceptor to share one institutional RCA with me and explicitly map the Swiss Cheese layers and their holes; and (3) carrying the model into my own dispensing practice — I will think of my verification step as one slice of cheese among many, not the last line of defense, so I am realistic about the system around me.

reflection_text:
This session covered "speak-up culture" and TeamSTEPPS as concrete tools for addressing one of the most stubborn problems in patient safety — the failure of team members to voice safety concerns to a more senior or more confident colleague. I learned the TeamSTEPPS techniques specifically designed to lower the cost of speaking up: CUS ("I'm Concerned, I'm Uncomfortable, this is a Safety issue"), the two-challenge rule (if your concern is dismissed once, you must voice it a second time before deferring), and DESC scripts for delivering structured feedback after a critical event. We also reviewed data showing that even experienced clinicians frequently fail to speak up when they see a senior colleague about to make an error — and that the gap is not knowledge or motivation but the absence of pre-rehearsed language to use under pressure.

This mattered to me personally because, as a student about to enter rotations where I will frequently be the most junior person in the room, I had been vaguely worried about "how to push back" without a concrete script. CUS and the two-challenge rule give me both permission and language. The class also made clear that an institution's speak-up culture is not built by training students alone — it requires senior clinicians to respond well when they are challenged, which I will now be alert to during rotations.

I plan to apply this by (1) memorizing the CUS script verbatim so I can use it under pressure rather than having to invent language in the moment; (2) asking my preceptor whether the institution has formal TeamSTEPPS training and if I can attend a session; and (3) when I observe a senior clinician respond well to a challenge from a junior team member, deliberately noting what they did so I can model that behavior when I am the senior person someday.

reflection_text:
Today's class focused on disclosure and apology after a harmful medical error — the practice of telling patients and families honestly what happened, why it happened, what the institution will do about it, and expressing genuine regret. I learned that the evidence-based components of effective disclosure are (1) timeliness (within 24–48 hours of identification), (2) involvement of the clinician most directly involved alongside an institutional representative, (3) clear factual description without speculation, (4) explicit apology, and (5) follow-up communication as the investigation progresses. We also reviewed the malpractice data — programs like the University of Michigan disclosure program have shown that early honest disclosure paradoxically reduces litigation, total claims costs, and time to resolution, in addition to its ethical importance.

What this learning shifted for me was the assumption that disclosure and legal self-protection are inevitably in tension. The data suggest the opposite. The ethical case for disclosure is straightforward; what surprised me was that the institutional case is also strong. I also realized that disclosure is a skill that requires practice — saying "I'm sorry" at the right moment in the right way is not innate, and clinicians who are not trained tend to under-disclose, speculate, or apologize defensively.

I will apply this by (1) asking my preceptor if the institution has a disclosure protocol and whether I can review one redacted disclosure summary to study the language and structure; (2) practicing a brief disclosure script with my preceptor in a low-stakes simulated scenario before I am ever in a real one; and (3) committing, if I am ever directly involved in an error that reaches a patient during my career, to participate in the disclosure rather than allow the institution to handle it alone — that is part of what I owe the patient.

reflection_text:
This session contextualized everything we have learned in the patient safety course by walking through the Institute of Medicine's 1999 report "To Err Is Human" and the field-defining shift it produced. I learned that the report estimated 44,000–98,000 preventable deaths per year in U.S. hospitals from medical errors and reframed safety as an emergent property of healthcare systems rather than the result of individual diligence. We reviewed the report's main recommendations — establish a national focus on safety, identify and learn from errors through reporting systems, raise standards through oversight bodies, and implement safety systems at the point of care — and tracked which have made progress (e.g., CMS-mandated reporting, accreditation standards) and which remain unfinished (e.g., interoperable error reporting, consistent disclosure practices).

This learning mattered because it gave me a coherent intellectual frame for the rest of the course. Without the IOM report's premise — that errors are produced by systems, not bad apples — none of the specific tools we have studied (RCA, Just Culture, Swiss Cheese, FMEA) make sense. It also clarified for me why patient safety is sometimes politically contested even within healthcare: "systems thinking" is sometimes resisted as deflecting responsibility from individuals, and being aware of that history will help me communicate about safety work more thoughtfully on rotations.

I will apply this by (1) reading the original IOM report in full before my first inpatient rotation; (2) committing, when I encounter resistance to systems-based safety thinking on rotation, to explain the underlying premise calmly rather than assume bad faith; and (3) using "To Err Is Human" as my internal frame whenever I am tempted to attribute a near miss to a single individual — first asking what about the system made the error possible.

reflection_text:
Today's class covered sentinel events as defined by The Joint Commission and the formal Root Cause Analysis (RCA) process triggered by them. I learned that a sentinel event is a patient safety event resulting in death, permanent harm, or severe temporary harm — and that TJC requires accredited organizations to conduct a formal RCA within 45 days, with a corrective action plan that includes accountable owners and outcome measures. We reviewed the most-reported sentinel events: wrong-site surgery, retained foreign objects, medication errors causing harm, falls with serious injury, and patient suicide. The class emphasized that the 45-day timeline is structural — it forces the organization to commit to a learning response before the event recedes from institutional memory.

What this clarified for me was the difference between an internal safety review and a regulatory-mandated RCA. The personal piece was understanding that, when I am eventually involved in a sentinel event, my role is not to wait for the report — it is to participate substantively in the RCA so that the lessons live in clinical practice, not just in the corrective action document. I had been imagining sentinel events as something that happens to an institution; the class reframed them as something an institution learns from when its people show up to the analysis.

I will apply this by (1) asking my hospital preceptor whether I can review one redacted RCA report so I understand the structure and depth of the analysis; (2) attending one RCA meeting if the institution permits observers, even on an unrelated topic; and (3) when I encounter a near miss during my career, treating it as a sentinel event in microcosm — applying the same five-whys analytical approach rather than waiting for actual harm to trigger formal review.

reflection_text:
Today's class covered workarounds and normalization of deviance as one of the most subtle and persistent threats to patient safety. I learned that a workaround is a routine bypass of a designed safety control — for example, scanning a printed copy of a wristband instead of the patient, or overriding an automated dispensing cabinet alert as a matter of habit. Diane Vaughan's term "normalization of deviance" describes the gradual social process by which a workaround becomes accepted: the workaround produces no harm in any single instance, the team becomes desensitized to it, and within months no one remembers the original safety rationale. We reviewed examples from healthcare and from the Challenger disaster, and discussed why workarounds are so hard to surface — they are usually invisible to leadership and embarrassing to frontline staff to discuss.

This was important to me because I had been thinking of safety culture as a matter of attitudes and intentions. The class reframed it as a matter of small accumulated habits, most of which form below the threshold of explicit team conversation. The personal piece was recognizing that I will be present at the formation of workarounds throughout my career — and that the most useful thing I can do is name them out loud the first time, before they normalize. That requires a kind of social courage I will need to practice before I can deploy it.

I will apply this by (1) on each rotation, keeping a private list of workarounds I observe and noting which ones a senior team member would have flagged at first sight; (2) practicing the language of naming a workaround respectfully in low-stakes moments (e.g., "I noticed we're scanning the printed wristband — is that a workflow we want?"); and (3) when I am eventually a preceptor or supervisor, treating the first-week questions a new hire asks as a free audit of my unit's workarounds — the things they ask about are usually the things we have stopped seeing.

reflection_text:
Today's class addressed three intersecting fatigue-related threats to patient safety: clinician fatigue from extended shifts and circadian disruption, alarm fatigue from over-frequent low-priority alarms, and decision fatigue from accumulated cognitive load over a shift. I learned that pharmacist error rates rise measurably after 8 hours of continuous verification work and that night-shift error rates are systematically higher than day-shift even after controlling for case mix. We reviewed the alarm-fatigue evidence — typical hospital monitor produces hundreds of alarms per patient per day, the great majority of which are non-actionable, with documented patient deaths from clinicians silencing alarms that turned out to be real. The instructor presented the engineering and policy responses: limiting consecutive shifts, mandatory rest periods, alarm-rationalization projects, and EHR changes that reduce low-value interruptions.

What this was important for was understanding that fatigue is not a personal weakness to be overcome with effort but a system property that the organization either designs around or doesn't. The personal piece was the data on pharmacist error rates after 8 hours — I had been treating my own fatigue as a willpower issue, and the class reframed it as a performance reality I should plan around (timing critical verifications earlier in shifts, taking actual breaks rather than skipping them).

I will apply this by (1) on rotations, observing how my own attention and accuracy trend across a shift and noting when I should escalate or defer; (2) asking my preceptor whether the institution has an alarm-rationalization project and what the data shows; and (3) when I am eventually responsible for staffing or scheduling, treating fatigue engineering as a primary job, not as a wellness initiative bolted on the side.

reflection_text:
Today's class focused on health literacy as a patient safety issue. I learned that approximately 36% of U.S. adults have basic or below-basic health literacy and that medication errors cluster heavily in this population — patients who cannot reliably read prescription labels, who misinterpret "take twice daily" as "take with two meals," or who lack the schema to understand what a side effect is. We reviewed the evidence-based safeguards: plain-language labeling (e.g., "take 1 pill in the morning and 1 pill at night" rather than "take 1 BID"), pictogram-supported instructions, teach-back at every counseling encounter, and use of the institution's professional medical interpreters rather than family members for limited-English-proficiency patients. The class also covered the AHRQ Universal Precautions toolkit, which assumes every patient may have low literacy — because identifying low-literacy patients by appearance or behavior is unreliable.

What this was important for was reframing patient counseling as a primary safety control rather than a courtesy. I had been thinking of counseling as the closing step of a transaction. The class made clear that for many patients, the counseling encounter is the only safety net between the prescription and a harmful self-administration error. The Universal Precautions framing also addressed something I had been quietly uncomfortable with — the temptation to assess literacy by appearance, which is both inaccurate and ethically problematic.

I will apply this by (1) committing to use teach-back at every counseling encounter on my retail rotation, even when the patient seems fluent and informed; (2) using only professional interpreters when the patient is limited-English-proficient and pushing back if the workflow tries to use family; and (3) reviewing the institution's prescription label conventions and proposing plain-language improvements wherever I see jargon.

reflection_text:
Today's session looked at post-discharge medication errors — events that happen after the patient leaves the hospital with their new medication regimen. I learned that the post-discharge period is one of the highest-risk periods in healthcare: patients are recovering, their medication list has often changed substantially during admission, the discharge counseling is frequently rushed, and the connection to the outpatient pharmacist is often absent. Published rates suggest 20-30% of patients experience a medication-related adverse event within 30 days of discharge, and a meaningful fraction lead to ED visits or readmission. We reviewed the interventions with the strongest evidence: meds-to-beds programs (filling discharge prescriptions before the patient leaves), post-discharge follow-up calls within 48-72 hours, and structured medication action plans given to the patient in writing.

What this was important for was recognizing that the discharge moment is not the end of inpatient care but the riskiest handoff in the entire hospitalization — and that pharmacy is the team that can most effectively close that gap. The personal piece was a clearer sense of what kind of pharmacy work I find most motivating; transitions-of-care work sits at the intersection of operational systems and direct patient care, and the data on its impact is strong.

I will apply this by (1) requesting a transitions-of-care rotation with a specific focus on post-discharge follow-up calls; (2) shadowing a meds-to-beds workflow at one institution to understand its operational details; and (3) keeping a personal log during my training of the specific medication-related issues that surface in post-discharge follow-up calls — that is the data I will use when I am eventually advocating for these services.

reflection_text:
Today's class introduced diagnostic error as one of the most under-recognized categories of patient safety harm — and one where pharmacy involvement is growing. I learned that the National Academy of Medicine's 2015 report Improving Diagnosis in Healthcare estimated that every adult will experience at least one meaningful diagnostic error in their lifetime, and that diagnostic errors contribute to about 10% of patient deaths. We discussed the cognitive contributors (anchoring, premature closure, availability bias) and the system contributors (test result follow-up gaps, EHR-mediated communication failures, fragmented care). The pharmacy angle was instructive: pharmacists frequently catch diagnostic errors at the point of dispensing — the reflexively prescribed antibiotic for a viral illness, the steroid without a clear indication, the duplicate therapy that suggests an unmade diagnosis. The instructor framed pharmacist intervention in suspected diagnostic errors as a developing area of practice rather than a settled one.

What this was important for was widening my understanding of patient safety beyond medication errors. I had been thinking of "safety" almost synonymously with "medication safety." The class reframed it as a broader field where pharmacists have particular leverage on diagnostic safety precisely because we see prescriptions across the entire care continuum. The personal piece was a heightened sense of professional latitude — when a prescription doesn't make clinical sense, asking the question is not stepping outside my role; it is fully within it.

reflection_text:
Today's class focused on implicit bias and equity as patient safety issues. I learned that patients of color, women, and patients with non-English primary language experience measurably higher rates of medication errors, delayed diagnoses, and inadequate pain management — and that these disparities persist after adjusting for clinical and socioeconomic factors, indicating bias as a contributor. We reviewed the evidence on implicit bias training (mixed — single sessions show small short-term effects), and on structural interventions that work better: standardized order sets that limit clinician discretion at decision points where bias operates, default opt-out interpreters, and explicit equity dashboards that show outcome disparities by demographic so they cannot be invisible. The class connected this material to the patient safety field's longstanding focus on system design — equity is a system property, not a question of individual goodwill.

What this was important for was recognizing that I cannot exempt myself from bias by being a well-intentioned person, and that the right response is structural rather than purely attitudinal. The personal piece was uncomfortable in productive ways — the data on disparities in pain management is severe, and as a future pharmacist I will be one of the checkpoints in that pathway. Recognizing that means I will need to deliberately use structural tools (standardized opioid pain assessment scripts, equity dashboards) rather than rely on my own judgment alone.

I will apply this by (1) reviewing the IHI Pursuing Equity initiative and the AHA's Health Equity Roadmap before my first rotation so I am fluent in the structural framing; (2) asking my hospital preceptor whether the institution stratifies any safety outcome data by demographic and, if not, naming that as a gap; and (3) committing to use teach-back and professional interpreter services with no exceptions on rotation, because those are the structural defaults that don't require my individual bias to be perfect.

reflection_text:
Today's session covered telehealth and remote prescribing safety as a patient safety frontier that grew rapidly during the COVID-19 pandemic and has not retreated. I learned that the safety profile of telehealth is mixed: for chronic disease management, telehealth shows comparable safety to in-person care; for acute conditions and for new patients, the absence of physical examination and unfamiliar prescribing context creates new failure modes — including controlled-substance e-prescribing without established prescriber-patient relationships, prescription cascades through platform-based DTC (direct-to-consumer) telehealth, and inadequate medication reconciliation. The class also covered the regulatory environment in flux: the Ryan Haight Act exception for telehealth controlled-substance prescribing has been repeatedly extended but not made permanent, and state boards are actively defining scope for remote pharmacy verification and compounding.

What this clarified for me was that telehealth is not a single thing — it ranges from established-relationship video visits with a primary care physician to single-encounter DTC platforms whose business model depends on prescription volume. The safety risks are concentrated at the platform-DTC end. The personal piece was a sharper sense that, as a community pharmacist, I will be the team member most able to identify problematic telehealth-DTC prescribing patterns at the point of dispensing — which is also a place where pharmacist refusal to dispense has real ethical and operational weight.

I will apply this by (1) reading the Federation of State Medical Boards telehealth guidance and my state board's pharmacy telehealth rules; (2) during my retail rotation, asking my preceptor about the site's policy for prescriptions originating from DTC telehealth platforms and what patterns trigger refusal; and (3) committing to escalate any telehealth prescription that doesn't fit the patient's clinical history, even if it is from a recognized platform — escalation is not the same as refusal, but it documents the concern.

reflection_text:
In today's class, I learned that USP Chapter <797> and USP Chapter <800> are the primary standards governing sterile compounding and hazardous drug handling in pharmacy settings. USP <797> establishes requirements for personnel training and competency assessment--including gloved fingertip sampling, media fill tests, and garbing competency evaluations--as well as environmental monitoring and beyond-use dating (BUDs) for sterile preparations based on compounding category and risk level. USP <800> addresses the safe handling of hazardous drugs as defined by the NIOSH Hazardous Drug List, requiring containment primary engineering controls (C-PECs), appropriate personal protective equipment, and defined procedures for spill management and waste disposal to protect healthcare workers from drug exposure. I also learned that ISO classifications define air quality in cleanroom environments: ISO 5 (critical zone), ISO 7 (buffer area), and ISO 8 (ante-area) each carry specific requirements for particulate counts and pressure differentials to maintain sterility.

These standards matter for my growth because they directly protect both patients and pharmacy staff. Before today, I understood that compounding had rules, but I had not appreciated how rigorously evidence-based and consequential those rules are. Contamination of sterile products has caused real patient harm--including the 2012 fungal meningitis outbreak linked to a compounding pharmacy in Massachusetts that resulted in over 750 illnesses and 64 deaths. Understanding that BUDs are assigned based on facility conditions and sterility testing--not arbitrary timelines--shifted how I think about precision in compounding practice.

Going forward, I will apply this learning at my internship by: (1) reviewing the site's standard operating procedures (SOPs) for sterile compounding and comparing them against current USP <797> and <800> requirements; (2) volunteering to observe environmental monitoring activities and competency assessments such as gloved fingertip sampling; and (3) completing all required garbing and media fill training before working independently in any cleanroom environment.

reflection_text:
Today's class provided a comprehensive overview of how the Drug Enforcement Administration (DEA) regulates controlled substances in pharmacy practice. I learned that drugs are classified into five federal schedules (Schedules I-V) based on medical utility and potential for abuse or physical or psychological dependence. Schedule I substances (e.g., heroin, LSD) have no accepted medical use and cannot be prescribed, while Schedules II-V are available by prescription with increasingly flexible requirements. Schedule II medications (e.g., oxycodone, fentanyl, amphetamines) require a new prescription each time--no refills are permitted--and must be ordered from suppliers using DEA Form 222 or the equivalent Controlled Substance Ordering System (CSOS). Schedules III-V allow up to five refills within six months. I also learned that all DEA-registered entities must conduct a biennial controlled substance inventory, maintain records for a minimum of two years, and use authorized pathways for disposal such as DEA-authorized collector sites or DEA Form 41 for reverse distribution. State prescription monitoring programs (PMPs) integrate with federal DEA oversight to help detect diversion patterns across prescribers and patients.

This is directly relevant to my growth as a future pharmacist because controlled substances represent one of the highest-risk areas in pharmacy practice. Dispensing errors, documentation failures, and diversion can have catastrophic consequences--patient harm, criminal prosecution, loss of DEA registration, and pharmacy closure. I had previously thought of controlled substance management primarily as administrative overhead--lots of forms and counts--but today's class reframed it as a critical public health and patient safety function. Given the ongoing opioid epidemic, I now understand more concretely how pharmacists serve as a key checkpoint in preventing diversion while still ensuring that patients with genuine clinical needs can access these medications.

To apply this in my practice, I will: (1) during my inpatient internship, review the facility's controlled substance policies and ask to shadow or assist with biennial inventory and daily count discrepancy resolution; (2) access my state's PMP system and practice running a patient's controlled substance history before filling a prescription to understand what patterns might trigger further review; and (3) ask my preceptor to walk me through the documentation and reporting process for a controlled substance discrepancy before I encounter one independently.

reflection_text: Today I learned that HIPAA--the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act--is composed of two key rules especially relevant to pharmacy practice: the Privacy Rule, which governs the use and disclosure of Protected Health Information (PHI), and the Security Rule, which applies specifically to electronic PHI (ePHI). PHI is defined as individually identifiable health information linked to any of 18 specific identifiers--such as name, address, dates of service, prescription numbers, or biometric data--that relates to a patient's health status, care, or payment for care. I learned that pharmacists must apply the minimum necessary standard when sharing patient information, meaning only the information required for the specific task at hand should be disclosed. HIPAA permits disclosure of PHI without patient authorization for Treatment, Payment, and Healthcare Operations (TPO), but other disclosures generally require a signed patient authorization. Violations carry tiered civil monetary penalties ranging from $100 to $50,000 per violation depending on culpability, as well as potential criminal liability for willful disclosures. This is important for my development as a pharmacist because patients must trust that their health information is handled with care. Without that trust, patients may withhold information that is critical to their safe care--putting themselves at risk. I will make sure to follow HIPAA standards when I work in pharmacy settings and be careful about how I share patient information.

reflection_text:
In today's session, I learned how HIPAA--the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act--applies to pharmacy practice in concrete, behavioral terms. The Privacy Rule protects Protected Health Information (PHI), defined as individually identifiable information linked to any of 18 specific identifiers (such as name, date of birth, address, or prescription number) that relates to a patient's health, treatment, or payment for care. The Security Rule extends these protections to electronic PHI (ePHI) and requires covered entities to implement administrative, physical, and technical safeguards. Pharmacists may share PHI without patient authorization for Treatment, Payment, and Healthcare Operations (TPO), but must apply the minimum necessary standard--sharing only what is required for the specific purpose. I also learned that pharmacies must execute Business Associate Agreements (BAAs) with third-party vendors that will have access to PHI, such as billing companies or pharmacy software vendors. Finally, HIPAA breach notification rules require covered entities to notify affected individuals, the Department of Health and Human Services (HHS), and--for breaches affecting more than 500 individuals in a state--relevant media outlets, all within 60 days of discovering the breach.

This resonated with me personally because early in my pharmacy experience, I observed a colleague reading out a patient's medication list at the front counter while other customers were nearby. At the time I felt uncomfortable but was unsure whether it was a HIPAA violation or simply poor practice. Today's class clarified that verbal disclosures of PHI in non-private settings where unauthorized individuals could overhear do constitute HIPAA violations. This reframed my understanding: HIPAA compliance is not primarily about securing computers or signing forms--it is an active behavioral standard embedded in every counter interaction, phone call, and counseling session.

Going forward, I will apply this by: (1) consistently moving to a private counseling area whenever discussing sensitive medication information with a patient, even under time pressure; (2) asking my preceptor at my internship to walk me through the site's breach investigation and notification protocol so I know what to do if I witness or accidentally cause a disclosure; and (3) reviewing the site's Business Associate Agreements to understand which vendors have access to patient data and for what purposes.

reflection_text:
Today I learned about the Drug Supply Chain Security Act (DSCSA), federal legislation enacted in 2013 that established a national framework for tracking and tracing prescription drug products throughout the pharmaceutical supply chain. Key elements include the requirement for trading partners--manufacturers, wholesale distributors, repackagers, and dispensers including pharmacies--to exchange three specific transaction documents: Transaction Information (TI), Transaction History (TH), and Transaction Statement (TS), collectively referred to as T3 documentation. I also learned about drug product serialization: every prescription drug package must carry a standardized Drug Product Identifier (DPI) encoded in a 2D data matrix barcode containing the National Drug Code (NDC), lot number, expiration date, and a unique serial number. Pharmacies are required to verify product identifiers for saleable returns before reintroducing them into inventory, investigate products flagged as suspect using a defined process, and report illegitimate products to the FDA. The law also set a target for an Enhanced Drug Distribution Security (EDDS) system enabling fully interoperable electronic tracing at the individual package level, though implementation has faced industry-wide delays.

This content shifted my understanding of the pharmacist's role in ways I did not expect. I had not previously thought of a drug's arrival at the pharmacy shelf as the endpoint of a regulated, documented chain of custody. Nor had I considered that pharmacists are active participants in supply chain security rather than just clinical care providers. Drug counterfeiting, diversion, and contamination are real threats that can directly harm patients--and the DSCSA exists to close the gaps where these threats can enter the supply chain. Knowing that I may one day need to quarantine a suspect product, investigate a discrepancy, or reject a saleable return that fails verification expanded my understanding of pharmacist responsibility well beyond the dispensing counter.

I will apply this learning by: (1) asking my preceptor at my internship to explain the site's process for receiving pharmaceutical shipments and how T3 documentation is reviewed and stored; (2) reviewing the FDA's published guidance on suspect and illegitimate product investigation procedures so I understand the procedural steps before I encounter one; and (3) asking the pharmacy team whether their dispensing system supports 2D barcode scanning for DSCSA verification and what the manual fallback process is when the system is unavailable.

reflection_text:
Today's class focused on the regulatory authority I will personally answer to throughout my career: the State Board of Pharmacy. I learned that, while federal agencies (FDA, DEA, HHS) set drug-product and substance-level regulations, the practice of pharmacy itself is licensed and disciplined at the state level. Each state's Board of Pharmacy promulgates regulations on scope of practice, technician-to-pharmacist ratios, pharmacist-in-charge responsibilities, continuing education requirements (typically 15–30 CE hours per year, with required hours in patient safety, opioid management, and immunization), and disciplinary processes for license violations. We also reviewed the Multistate Pharmacy Licensure Compact and the difference between license reciprocity by score transfer (NAPLEX score transfer) and reciprocity by examination, which affects practical career mobility.

This learning mattered to me because I had been thinking of "pharmacy regulation" mostly in federal terms — DEA, FDA, HIPAA — and had not appreciated that the state board has the most direct day-to-day authority over how I practice and whether I keep my license. Realizing that the rules for things like remote verification, technician scope, and CE compliance vary by state changed how I think about taking a job out of state, and clarified that part of my professional development is learning my state board's regulations as carefully as I am learning federal ones.

I will apply this by (1) downloading and reading my state's Pharmacy Practice Act and accompanying regulations before I graduate, with particular attention to the technician scope, remote verification rules, and CE requirements; (2) signing up for my state board's email list so I receive regulatory updates and disciplinary actions, which I will read for the lessons-learned content; and (3) building a personal CE-tracking spreadsheet from year one of practice so I never end a renewal cycle short on hours or topic-required content.

reflection_text:
Today's session covered Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategies (REMS) programs as the FDA's mechanism for ensuring that the benefits of certain high-risk drugs continue to outweigh their risks after approval. I learned that REMS are not optional safety suggestions — they are FDA-required conditions of approval, and dispensing a REMS drug outside the program is non-compliant for the pharmacy. REMS elements can include Medication Guides, Communication Plans, and Elements to Assure Safe Use (ETASU) such as required prescriber certification, mandatory patient enrollment, lab monitoring (e.g., absolute neutrophil count for clozapine), and certified-pharmacy registration. We also reviewed the move toward shared system REMS (e.g., the Transmucosal Immediate-Release Fentanyl REMS, the iPLEDGE REMS for isotretinoin) where multiple manufacturers participate in a unified program.

This shifted my picture of post-market drug regulation. I had thought of FDA approval as a single binary event. The REMS framework reframes it as a conditional approval where the manufacturer accepts ongoing risk-management obligations, and the pharmacist becomes one of the points where those obligations are operationalized. Personally, this clarified that REMS errors are not minor administrative slips — failing to verify clozapine ANC or filling an isotretinoin prescription without a current iPLEDGE confirmation is both unsafe for the patient and a regulatory violation for the pharmacy.

I will apply this by (1) asking my retail and inpatient preceptors to walk me through how their site handles three current REMS programs (likely clozapine, isotretinoin, and TIRF) so I see the actual workflow rather than the abstract rule; (2) maintaining a personal reference list of the active REMS programs relevant to my practice setting; and (3) committing, when I encounter a REMS-controlled prescription, to verify the REMS element rather than assume the prescriber's office has already done so — the pharmacy is a co-equal responsible party.

reflection_text:
Today's class drew the distinction between 503A pharmacies (traditional patient-specific compounding) and 503B outsourcing facilities (office-stock compounding under cGMP), and the regulatory implications of each. I learned that 503A pharmacies compound on receipt of a prescription for an identified patient, are regulated primarily by state boards under USP <797> and <800>, and may not produce drugs that are essentially copies of commercially available products. 503B outsourcing facilities, created by the Drug Quality and Security Act after the 2012 NECC fungal meningitis outbreak, register with the FDA, follow current Good Manufacturing Practice (cGMP), and may produce sterile compounds in bulk for office stock without patient-specific prescriptions, subject to FDA inspection. We also reviewed the practical implications: a hospital sourcing decisions about which compounded products to bring in-house versus outsource is a real-money decision that turns on this distinction.

What this learning crystallized for me was why the 503A/503B framework exists at all — it is the regulatory response to a real, recent harm event (NECC) that killed 64 people. The distinction is not bureaucratic; it is the way the system tries to ensure that bulk sterile compounding carries manufacturer-grade oversight while preserving the ability of community and hospital pharmacies to compound for specific patients. I had previously thought of compounding regulation as monolithic; understanding the two-tier structure clarified the trade-offs that hospital pharmacy directors actually make.

I will apply this by (1) asking my hospital preceptor whether the site uses 503B outsourcing for any sterile compounded products and, if so, how the supplier was vetted; (2) reading at least one FDA inspection report (Form 483) of a 503B facility to understand what cGMP findings actually look like; and (3) being able, by the end of my advanced rotations, to articulate coherently when 503A versus 503B sourcing is appropriate so I can contribute to that conversation as a new pharmacist.

reflection_text:
Today's class covered the FDA drug approval pathway and post-market pharmacovigilance, with attention to the pharmacist's role at each stage. I learned the structure of the IND/NDA process — preclinical, Phase I (safety, small healthy-volunteer), Phase II (efficacy, dose-finding), Phase III (large randomized comparison), NDA submission and FDA review — and the difference between standard, priority, breakthrough, and accelerated approval designations. We also reviewed Phase IV / post-market surveillance: REMS, FDA Adverse Event Reporting System (FAERS), MedWatch reports, manufacturer Periodic Safety Update Reports, and label updates triggered by real-world signals. The instructor walked us through several real cases (rofecoxib, rosiglitazone, troglitazone) where serious risks emerged only after widespread post-market use.

This was important because I had implicitly thought of FDA approval as the endpoint of drug evaluation. Today's class made clear that the pre-approval trials, while necessary, cannot detect rare adverse events, long-latency harms, or interactions that surface only at population scale. Pharmacovigilance is the actual safety net, and pharmacists submit a substantial portion of all MedWatch reports — meaning my future MedWatch submissions are not optional good citizenship but a structural part of how the FDA learns about drugs after approval.

I will apply this by (1) reading the FDA MedWatch reporting form before my first rotation so the form is not new to me when I encounter a reportable event; (2) asking each preceptor about the institution's process for routing suspected ADRs and committing to file at least one MedWatch report during my rotations under preceptor supervision; and (3) building a habit, when I see a new drug being prescribed, of looking up its approval pathway and any post-market label updates so I am thinking about its safety profile in context rather than as a fixed package insert.

reflection_text:
Today's class covered USP Chapter <795>, the standard for non-sterile compounding — a category that includes oral suspensions, ointments, capsules, and other dosage forms produced for patients in retail and specialty compounding pharmacies. I learned that <795> establishes requirements for facilities (a designated compounding area, appropriate ventilation, controlled humidity), personnel training and competency documentation, master formulation records, compounding records that are patient-specific, and beyond-use date assignment based on the type of preparation and the available stability data. Unlike <797>, <795> does not require ISO-classified cleanroom space, but it does require a clean, uncluttered space distinct from general dispensing flow. The 2023 revision introduced more explicit BUD limits — 14 days for aqueous oral preparations stored under refrigeration, 35 days for water-containing oral preparations under specific conditions, and 90 days for solid dosage forms — anchored to documented stability evidence rather than tradition.

What this was important for was recognizing that <795> is the standard most community pharmacists will operate under daily. I had been studying <797> and <800> as the high-stakes standards while treating <795> as a default. The class corrected that — non-sterile compounding errors are common, rarely make headlines individually, and accumulate into substantial patient harm at the population level. Pediatric oral suspensions in particular are a high-risk <795> category I had been underweighting in my mental map of compounding safety.

I will apply this by (1) reading the 2023 USP <795> revision in full before my retail rotation, with attention to the BUD tables; (2) asking my preceptor to walk me through the site's master formulation record for a common oral suspension and identify any gaps from the new standard; and (3) when I encounter a non-sterile compound on rotation, treating the BUD assignment as a clinical decision requiring documented stability evidence rather than a default value.

reflection_text:
Today's class introduced the 340B Drug Pricing Program, a federal program that requires drug manufacturers to provide significant outpatient drug discounts to specific safety-net providers — including federally qualified health centers, Ryan White HIV/AIDS Program grantees, disproportionate share hospitals, and certain critical access hospitals. I learned that 340B was created in 1992 to stretch limited federal resources by enabling covered entities to use the savings to expand services to underserved patients. The compliance framework is exacting: covered entities must maintain auditable records that 340B-priced drugs went only to eligible patients, must avoid duplicate discounts (drugs purchased at 340B prices cannot also be billed at Medicaid rebate rates), and must accept HRSA audits. We discussed both the program's success in expanding access and the controversy about whether all covered entities use savings as the statute intended.

What this was important for was understanding that the pharmacy supply chain has a parallel financial architecture I had not previously seen. The 340B compliance framework places significant operational responsibility on the pharmacist, particularly around contract pharmacy arrangements and patient-eligibility determinations. The personal piece was recognizing that if I work at a 340B covered entity early in my career, the compliance obligations are substantial enough that I should learn the program in detail rather than treat it as something the management team handles.

I will apply this by (1) reviewing the HRSA 340B program manual before any rotation at a 340B-eligible site; (2) asking my preceptor to walk me through the patient-eligibility determination workflow at the site, including how the pharmacist contributes; and (3) reading at least two recent HRSA 340B audit findings to understand what compliance failures actually look like, so I am prepared to recognize them.

reflection_text:
Today's session focused on telepharmacy — the practice of providing pharmacy services remotely, typically via video and electronic dispensing — and the regulatory framework that governs it. I learned that telepharmacy is primarily state-regulated, with significant variation: some states explicitly authorize telepharmacy with specific dispensing-cabinet arrangements (North Dakota was an early adopter), while others restrict it to remote verification only. The common regulatory elements are pharmacist licensure in the state of patient location, technician supervision rules that often include video monitoring requirements, and dispensing-equipment specifications. We also discussed the practical model: a single pharmacist remotely supervising several rural pharmacy locations staffed by technicians — which expands access in underserved areas but requires rigorous policy to maintain pharmacist judgment in dispensing.

What this clarified was that telepharmacy is one of the fastest-changing areas of pharmacy regulation and one where state board action is more consequential than federal. I had been thinking of remote work in pharmacy as a workflow choice; the class reframed it as a regulated practice modality whose rules I need to track in any state I practice. The personal piece was recognizing that career flexibility increasingly depends on fluency with telepharmacy regulations and the tools that operationalize them.

I will apply this by (1) reading my state board's telepharmacy regulations before graduation, with attention to remote verification rules; (2) asking each retail and hospital preceptor whether their site uses any remote verification or telepharmacy and how the workflow is regulated; and (3) following the National Association of Boards of Pharmacy telepharmacy white papers so I am tracking the field as it evolves rather than relearning it later.

reflection_text:
Today's class covered Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) requirements that apply to pharmacy practice — particularly hazardous drug handling, bloodborne pathogen exposure, and ergonomics. I learned that OSHA's Hazard Communication Standard requires Safety Data Sheets (SDS) for all hazardous chemicals in the workplace, including the cleaning agents and reagents used in compounding. The Bloodborne Pathogens Standard applies to pharmacy staff who administer immunizations and requires specific exposure control plans, post-exposure protocols, and Hepatitis B vaccination availability. The hazardous drug handling requirements intersect with USP <800> but extend further into engineering controls (C-PECs), administrative controls (training, medical surveillance for exposed employees), and PPE specifications. The class also covered ergonomics — pharmacy is one of the higher-risk occupations for repetitive strain injury due to constant standing and counter work.

What this was important for was recognizing that pharmacy regulation extends beyond drug-product rules into workplace safety in ways I had not fully appreciated. The personal piece was a clearer sense that, when I am eventually a pharmacy manager or PIC, OSHA compliance is part of the job rather than an HR concern that someone else handles.

I will apply this by (1) reviewing the OSHA Pharmaceutical Industry page and the bloodborne pathogen standard before my immunization-providing retail rotation; (2) asking my preceptor where the site's SDS binder is and confirming I can locate one for a common cleaning agent; and (3) completing the institution's annual bloodborne pathogen training in full rather than skimming it, so I have the actual procedural knowledge if I experience a needle-stick.

reflection_text:
Today's session covered pharmacy fraud and abuse — specifically the Anti-Kickback Statute (AKS), the Stark Law (physician self-referral), and the False Claims Act — and how each applies to pharmacy practice. I learned that AKS prohibits the exchange of anything of value to induce or reward referrals for federally reimbursable services; that Stark prohibits physicians from referring Medicare or Medicaid patients for designated health services to entities the physician (or immediate family) has a financial relationship with; and that the False Claims Act creates liability for submitting or causing the submission of false or fraudulent claims to federal health programs. We reviewed pharmacy-specific examples: a chain pharmacy offering gift cards for transferring prescriptions could violate AKS; a dispensing physician with a financial interest in a specific pharmacy could create Stark issues; and a pharmacy billing for medications not actually dispensed creates False Claims liability with treble damages.

What this was important for was recognizing that pharmacy regulation is not only about drug products — it includes the financial integrity of the prescription transaction. I had been thinking of fraud-and-abuse compliance as something for billing departments to manage.
